reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a differentiable rendering method that builds connection between the 3D model and 2D perspective appearance. Different from previous methods, the differentiable rendering is formulated into an optimization problem. Two computer vision tasks, including pose estimation and 3D shape from color image, are evaluated with the proposed renderer and compared to SoftRas [22].

Main Review:
Strength:
- The performance for pose estimation outperforms existing methods.
- The formulation seems novel.

Weakness:
- Could the proposed formulation be applied to other 3D shape representations, e.g. point cloud, implicit? If so, an experiment should be added. If not, then better to mention this is a mesh-specific technic.

- The algorithm seems to be computational costy, e.g. 1 min per image. Would this heavily affect the training efficiency? While comparing to other methods, e.g. SoftRas, the run-time efficiency and hyper-parameters, e.g. number of iteration, training epoch, should be mentioned. Otherwise the comparison may not be on a fair basis.

- Keeping the boundary differentiable is one of the main issues in existing differentiable rendering technics, which is also argued mainly in this paper. However it is not clear how this is improved by the proposed method. From Fig 2 and 4, it seems the boundary area has obvious artifacts. Also some experiments should be performed to highlight the improvements over occlusion boundary.

- The performance on 3D shape prediction does not outperform existing approaches. 

Overall, despite a novel formulation, the improvements over existing approaches are not clear. As shown in the experiment, the propose method does not necessarily produce more accurate rendering or support better for vision tasks. Considering that the proposed tech is also quite slow, the overall advantage over existing approaches is not very clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No comment.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper introduces a differentiable renderer that links 3D models to their 2D perspective projections, framing differentiable rendering explicitly as an optimization problem rather than following prior formulations. The method is evaluated on two computer vision tasks—pose estimation and 3D shape recovery from a single color image—and compared against SoftRas [22].

Main Review:
Strengths:
- The approach achieves superior performance on pose estimation relative to existing methods.
- The optimization-based formulation appears original.

Weaknesses:
- It is unclear whether the proposed formulation extends beyond meshes to other 3D representations such as point clouds or implicit surfaces. If it does, an additional experiment should be included; if it does not, the paper should state explicitly that the technique is mesh-specific.
- The method seems computationally heavy (approximately 1 minute per image). This raises concerns about training efficiency. A fair comparison to SoftRas requires reporting runtime and relevant hyperparameters (e.g., number of iterations, training epochs); otherwise, the comparisons may not be equitable.
- Handling differentiability at object boundaries is a central challenge in differentiable rendering and is also emphasized in this work, yet the manuscript does not make clear how the proposed method improves this aspect. Figures 2 and 4 suggest visible artifacts near boundaries. Additional experiments are needed to specifically demonstrate improvements around occlusion boundaries.
- The 3D shape reconstruction results do not surpass the current state of the art.

Overall assessment:
Although the formulation is novel, its practical advantages over existing methods are not well established. The experiments do not convincingly show gains in rendering fidelity or downstream vision performance, and the approach is relatively slow. Taken together, the benefits over prior work remain ambiguous.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
No comment.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: NAS-Bench-x11 and the Power of Learning Curves

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes another benchmark dataset for NAS, NAS-Bench-x11. Compared to the original benchmarks NAS-Bench-101, NAS-Bench-301 and NAS-Bench-NLP, NAS-Bench-x11 contains the full training
information for each candidate architecture especially the learning curves so that it allows multi-fidelity NAS algorithms

NAS-Bench-x11 is a surrogate benchmark, which trains a surrogate model to predict the learning curve of the vast amount of candidate architectures. Prediction of the learning curve can be accomplished by singular value decomposition and noise modeling.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:

The paper is well motivated and clearly written. The paper is clear about limitations too.

I think this paper will be valuable to the community.  The created NAS-bench-x11 contains the full training information of different architectures, which may make it more convenient to analyse NAS algorithms and promote further NAS algorithm design. The idea of predicting the learning curve is very interesting to me. 

The authors conducted extensive experiments to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.  

Weakness:

This paper is a completion of the previously created benchmarks. Providing learning curves is also considered before in NAS-Bench-201, which weakens the novelty of this paper. So I recommend a weak acceptence.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces a new NAS benchmark, NAS-Bench-x11. In contrast to prior benchmarks such as NAS-Bench-101, NAS-Bench-301, and NAS-Bench-NLP, NAS-Bench-x11 provides complete training trajectories for every candidate architecture, including full learning curves, thereby supporting multi-fidelity NAS methods.

NAS-Bench-x11 is a surrogate-based benchmark: a surrogate model is trained to predict learning curves for a large set of architectures. The learning-curve prediction relies on singular value decomposition together with noise modeling.

Main Review:
Strengths:
- The manuscript is well motivated, clearly presented, and explicitly acknowledges its limitations.
- The resource is likely to be useful for the community. By offering full training information across architectures, NAS-Bench-x11 should simplify the analysis of NAS algorithms and help spur new method development. I find the approach of modeling entire learning curves particularly compelling.
- The experimental evaluation is extensive and supports the method’s effectiveness.

Weaknesses:
- The contribution mainly extends earlier benchmarks rather than breaking new ground. Learning curves have already been included in NAS-Bench-201, which diminishes the novelty. Accordingly, I lean toward a weak acceptance.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
Yes.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Decision:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: Existing NAS benchmarks often only include the architecture performance at the final or some selected checkpoint epochs. However, per-epoch information is essential for developing and evaluating multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. This work proposed to fit a surrogate model to predict the learning curve given an architecture encoding. Utilizing the proposed predictor, the author extends three existing benchmarks NASBench-101, NASBench-301, and NASBench-NLP to include per-epoch accuracies. Further, to demonstrate the necessity of developing multi-fidelity algorithms for NAS, the author also describes a procedure to extend the single-fidelity algorithms to multi-fidelity ones by using learning curve extrapolation to filter out candidate proposals with partial training. Despite possible room for improvements on the performance of the learning curve predictor used, the proposed method would enable researchers to develop and rapidly test multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. I vote for acceptance.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The contribution of this paper is relevant to the NAS community and would accelerate research on multi-fidelity NAS algorithms.
2. The performance of the proposed learning curve predictor model is overall satisfactory.
3. The author adopted the proposed method to extend three existing benchmarks to include per-epoch information.

Weakness:
1. The author reported ranking correlation and KL divergence between the predicted learning curve and ground truth. The numbers seem reasonable, but the errors are still non-negligible. It is unclear how misleading these errors would be if someone tries to develop multi-fidelity algorithms by querying the proposed extended benchmark. How would the existing multi-fidelity NAS algorithms perform under the proposed benchmark, over ground-truth data? This might be doable for NASBench201 or NASBench111 (the version created without using information about the given 4 checkpoint epochs), as they provide ground-truth accuracies on at least some checkpoints?

Comments and Questions:
1. For NASBench-NLP, using the accuracy from the first few epochs as features significantly improves the learning curve predictor's performance. Can we also expect visible improvements on other spaces, if the info of the first few epochs is also provided?

2. You mentioned in para 355 that future surrogate works should save full learning curve info to avoid the need for any extra extensions. But surrogate benchmarks like NASBench-301 only train a small portion of the architectures fully, and the large majority part of the search space will be benched by the predictor. So it seems that such surrogate benchmark would still need to be extended with learning curve prediction anyway. Could you elaborate more on this?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
Existing NAS benchmarks often only include the architecture performance at the final or some selected checkpoint epochs. However, per-epoch information is essential for developing and evaluating multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. This work proposed to fit a surrogate model to predict the learning curve given an architecture encoding. Utilizing the proposed predictor, the author extends three existing benchmarks NASBench-101, NASBench-301, and NASBench-NLP to include per-epoch accuracies. Further, to demonstrate the necessity of developing multi-fidelity algorithms for NAS, the author also describes a procedure to extend the single-fidelity algorithms to multi-fidelity ones by using learning curve extrapolation to filter out candidate proposals with partial training. Despite possible room for improvements on the performance of the learning curve predictor used, the proposed method would enable researchers to develop and rapidly test multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. I vote for acceptance.

Main Review:
Strength:
1. The contribution of this paper is relevant to the NAS community and would accelerate research on multi-fidelity NAS algorithms.
2. The performance of the proposed learning curve predictor model is overall satisfactory.
3. The author adopted the proposed method to extend three existing benchmarks to include per-epoch information.

Weakness:
1. The author reported ranking correlation and KL divergence between the predicted learning curve and ground truth. The numbers seem reasonable, but the errors are still non-negligible. It is unclear how misleading these errors would be if someone tries to develop multi-fidelity algorithms by querying the proposed extended benchmark. How would the existing multi-fidelity NAS algorithms perform under the proposed benchmark, over ground-truth data? This might be doable for NASBench201 or NASBench111 (the version created without using information about the given 4 checkpoint epochs), as they provide ground-truth accuracies on at least some checkpoints?

Comments and Questions:
1. For NASBench-NLP, using the accuracy from the first few epochs as features significantly improves the learning curve predictor's performance. Can we also expect visible improvements on other spaces, if the info of the first few epochs is also provided?

2. You mentioned in para 355 that future surrogate works should save full learning curve info to avoid the need for any extra extensions. But surrogate benchmarks like NASBench-301 only train a small portion of the architectures fully, and the large majority part of the search space will be benched by the predictor. So it seems that such surrogate benchmark would still need to be extended with learning curve prediction anyway. Could you elaborate more on this?

reviews.text:
Summary:
Many NAS benchmarks report only final performance or a few checkpoint results, which is insufficient for building and assessing multi-fidelity NAS methods that rely on per-epoch trajectories. This paper trains a surrogate model that predicts full learning curves from architecture encodings. Using this predictor, the author augments NASBench-101, NASBench-301, and NASBench-NLP with per-epoch accuracy data. To further motivate multi-fidelity NAS, the paper outlines how to convert single-fidelity algorithms into multi-fidelity variants by extrapolating learning curves and pruning candidates after partial training. While the predictor could likely be improved, the approach provides a practical way for researchers to design and quickly evaluate multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. I recommend acceptance.

Main Review:

Strengths:
- The contribution is timely and valuable for the NAS community and will likely speed up progress on multi-fidelity NAS.
- The proposed learning-curve predictor achieves generally solid performance.
- The method is used to extend three widely used benchmarks with per-epoch information.

Weaknesses:
- The paper reports rank correlation and KL divergence between predicted and true learning curves. Although the metrics look reasonable, the errors are still notable. It remains unclear how these discrepancies might bias the development of multi-fidelity algorithms that rely on the extended benchmark. How would current multi-fidelity NAS methods fare on the proposed benchmark compared to ground truth? This seems testable on NASBench201 or NASBench111 (the version created without using the four provided checkpoint epochs), which offer ground-truth accuracies for at least some checkpoints.

Comments and Questions:
- For NASBench-NLP, including accuracies from the first few epochs as features substantially improves learning-curve prediction. Would similar gains be expected in other search spaces if early-epoch data were available there as well?
- In paragraph 355, you suggest future surrogate benchmarks should store complete learning curves to avoid additional extensions. However, in surrogate setups like NASBench-301, only a small subset of architectures are fully trained and most entries are produced by the surrogate. Wouldn’t such benchmarks still require learning-curve prediction for the majority of the space? Please clarify.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

Original Decision (Rating):
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: The authors describe a mechanism to predict learning curves which they use to generate surrogate benchmarks from existing NAS-Bench versions without learning curves. The authors explore different ways to create learning curve embeddings, different ways to predict these embeddings using the architecture representation as an input and different ways to model the training noise. Furthermore, the authors discuss a short study on using learning curve extrapolation in context of speeding-up NAS.
reviews.main_review:
The authors provide a good description of their method, alternative ways to solve each problem faced and support their decisions with empirical evidence.

My main concern is about the usefulness of this surrogate benchmark which has problems as partly discussed by the authors. It remains unclear what impact these problems would have.

The noise modelling in this paper leads to artifacts we would not see in real learning curves. First, it is very unlikely that the noise is architecture- and hyperparameter-independent. Second, given just the very few examples in Figure 1 we observe that the predicted learning curve in early epochs can already exceed to highest value of the entire learning curve (possibly due to very high noise levels).
In general, all surrogate benchmarks have the problem that they introduce some sort of bias which will not match what you would see in reality. Therefore, these datasets are more similar to synthetic datasets than real ones. They might be nice to prototype but I wouldn’t use them to evaluate NAS methods.

The experiment on combining learning curve extrapolation is a nice reminder for the NAS community to keep these approaches in mind when comparing different approaches. However, it provides no novelty since these methods have been used in the context of NAS already (see [1]).
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

Summary:
The authors describe a mechanism to predict learning curves which they use to generate surrogate benchmarks from existing NAS-Bench versions without learning curves. The authors explore different ways to create learning curve embeddings, different ways to predict these embeddings using the architecture representation as an input and different ways to model the training noise. Furthermore, the authors discuss a short study on using learning curve extrapolation in context of speeding-up NAS.

Main Review:
The authors provide a good description of their method, alternative ways to solve each problem faced and support their decisions with empirical evidence.

My main concern is about the usefulness of this surrogate benchmark which has problems as partly discussed by the authors. It remains unclear what impact these problems would have.

The noise modelling in this paper leads to artifacts we would not see in real learning curves. First, it is very unlikely that the noise is architecture- and hyperparameter-independent. Second, given just the very few examples in Figure 1 we observe that the predicted learning curve in early epochs can already exceed to highest value of the entire learning curve (possibly due to very high noise levels).
In general, all surrogate benchmarks have the problem that they introduce some sort of bias which will not match what you would see in reality. Therefore, these datasets are more similar to synthetic datasets than real ones. They might be nice to prototype but I wouldn’t use them to evaluate NAS methods.

The experiment on combining learning curve extrapolation is a nice reminder for the NAS community to keep these approaches in mind when comparing different approaches. However, it provides no novelty since these methods have been used in the context of NAS already (see [1]).

Yes

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a method to predict learning curves and use them to build surrogate benchmarks from NAS-Bench variants that lack learning curves. The authors examine multiple strategies for constructing learning-curve embeddings, for predicting these embeddings from architectural encodings, and for modeling training noise. They also include a brief study on leveraging learning curve extrapolation to accelerate NAS.

Main Review:
The paper clearly explains the approach, presents alternative design choices for each component, and backs the selected choices with empirical evidence.

My primary reservation concerns the practical value of the resulting surrogate benchmark, which has issues the authors partially acknowledge. The consequences of these issues remain uncertain.

The noise model introduces artifacts that would not occur in real learning curves. Specifically, it is implausible that noise would be independent of both architecture and hyperparameters. Moreover, even in the few examples shown in Figure 1, the predicted curves can surpass the eventual peak performance early in training, likely due to overly large noise levels.
More broadly, surrogate benchmarks inevitably inject biases that diverge from real-world behavior, making them closer to synthetic datasets than authentic ones. They may be useful for quick prototyping, but I would not rely on them to assess NAS methods.

The experiment on combining learning curve extrapolation serves as a helpful reminder for the NAS community to consider such techniques when comparing methods. However, it does not contribute novelty, as these ideas have already been applied within NAS (see [1]).

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

Original Decision (Rating)
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: Existing NAS benchmark datasets (e.g., NAS-Bench-101) do not provide full training information of architectures, which make it infeasible to evaluate the multi-fidelity algorithms (e.g., learning curve extrapolation). To address these issues, this paper create surrogate benchmark datasets, namely NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311 and NAS-Bench-NLP11, with singular value decomposition and noise modeling methods. Extensive experimental results with the full training information on these datasets show the superiority of the proposed method.
reviews.main_review:
## Positive points:
1. The paper presents a method to extend the existing single-fidelity benchmarks to multi-fidelity ones with full training information.
2. The authors create NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11 benchmark datasets, which may be beneficial to develop new NAS algorithm.

## Negative points:
1. Does the proposed method apply to real world NAS application? In practice, given a dataset, we may have no available training information of any architectures. In this case, how to apply the proposed method? It would be limited if the proposed method is only able to extend the existing NAS benchmarks.
2. In line 220, why do the authors sample architectures from BANANAS, local search and regularized evolution?
3. In line 225, the authors mention that the method is able to improve the performance with the accuracies from the tabular benchmark (at epochs 4, 12, 36, 108) as additional features. More details of this should be provided.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

Summary:
Existing NAS benchmark datasets (e.g., NAS-Bench-101) do not provide full training information of architectures, which make it infeasible to evaluate the multi-fidelity algorithms (e.g., learning curve extrapolation). To address these issues, this paper create surrogate benchmark datasets, namely NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311 and NAS-Bench-NLP11, with singular value decomposition and noise modeling methods. Extensive experimental results with the full training information on these datasets show the superiority of the proposed method.

reviews.text:
Summary:
Most existing NAS benchmarks (e.g., NAS-Bench-101) lack complete training trajectories for architectures, which prevents fair evaluation of multi-fidelity methods such as learning-curve extrapolation. This paper proposes surrogate multi-fidelity benchmarks—NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11—constructed using singular value decomposition and noise modeling. The authors report extensive experiments leveraging full training information on these datasets and claim the proposed approach outperforms alternatives.

Strengths:
- The paper introduces a way to transform single-fidelity benchmarks into multi-fidelity ones with full training traces.
- The release of NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11 could facilitate the development of new NAS methods.

Weaknesses:
- Practical applicability to real-world NAS is unclear. In practice, for a new dataset, there may be no training information for any architectures. How would the method be used in that scenario? If it only augments existing NAS benchmarks, the scope appears limited.
- At line 220, the rationale for sampling architectures from BANANAS, local search, and regularized evolution is not explained. Please justify these choices.
- At line 225, the paper states that adding accuracies from the tabular benchmark at epochs 4, 12, 36, and 108 as features improves performance. More implementation and methodological details are needed here.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The manuscript appropriately discusses limitations and potential negative societal impacts.

title: Compressed Video Contrastive Learning

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a self-supervised method for compressed video representation learning, which helps save both storage and computation.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:

- The proposed method directly uses RGB and motion vectors in the compressed video for efficient representation learning.
- Experimental results show that the proposed method reduces the total (inference) time and storage, and achieves marginal improvement compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Weakness:

- Adding temporal learning objectives such as changing the temporal order of RGB frames or motion vectors within a clip ( as in IMRNet ), which is missing from the proposed method, would greatly help learning video representation.
- Modeling of the proposed method mostly applies the InfoNCE loss, only selecting of positive and negative samples are updated for RGB and motion vectors.
- As shown in table 1, the computation overhead largely comes from the preprocessing time. Though not an apple-to-apple comparison, parallel re-encoding for IMRNet may largely mitigate the overhead?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The reviewer would encourage the authors to discuss limitations and impacts of the proposed method, as mentioned in the 'Main Review'.

Main Review:
Strength:

Weakness:

- Adding temporal learning objectives such as changing the temporal order of RGB frames or motion vectors within a clip ( as in IMRNet ), which is missing from the proposed method, would greatly help learning video representation.
- Modeling of the proposed method mostly applies the InfoNCE loss, only selecting of positive and negative samples are updated for RGB and motion vectors.

Limitations And Societal Impact:

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper introduces a self-supervised approach for learning representations directly from compressed video, aiming to reduce both storage needs and computational costs.

Main Review:
Strengths:
- The method leverages RGB and motion vectors extracted from compressed streams to enable efficient representation learning.
- Empirical results indicate reduced overall inference time and storage, with slight performance gains relative to state-of-the-art baselines.

Weaknesses:
- The approach lacks temporal learning objectives (e.g., reordering RGB frames or motion vectors within a clip as done in IMRNet), which could substantially strengthen temporal representation learning.
- The modeling primarily relies on the InfoNCE loss; the main novelty lies in how positive and negative samples are selected for RGB and motion vectors rather than in the loss design itself.
- Table 1 suggests that preprocessing dominates the computational overhead. While not a strictly fair comparison, parallel re-encoding in IMRNet could substantially alleviate this overhead.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The authors are encouraged to explicitly discuss the method’s limitations and potential societal impacts, as noted in the main review.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Original Decision (Rating):
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The authors propose a self-supervised learning (SSL) method for learning strong visual representations based on compressed videos. Compared with prior work, which sees videos in RGB only, the proposed method is more efficient and leverages the motion information that's already accessible in compressed videos. The authors propose to use a contrastive learning method to supervise on kind of modality with correspondence of another modality. On multiple datasets (mainly UCF and HMDB), good performance is achieved, with both linear probing and transfer learning settings.
reviews.main_review:
[originality: medium-high]
 - The idea of using compressed videos for self-supervised learning is novel/original. Most prior work that operates on compressed videos focues on supervised learning setting only. The implementation of this idea is standard/expected (this is not a drawback).

[quality: medium-high]
 - The paper covers useful ablation study that I'd expect. In particular, the authors demonstrate the usefulness of the compressed-video modality (MV) compared to RGB only (Table 3). Compared to prior work, the performance is also strong while being significantly more efficient. The ablation study that compares with optical flow and low-res optical flow is also interesting (Table 2).

[clarity: high]
 - I think this paper is overall clearly writen. One question about implementation details: How is the similarity calculated in the hard-positive mining procedure?

[significance: medium-high]
 - The area of SSL draws a lot of attention in our community and is of great potential. I think this work extends our knowledge from a new dimension with the use of compressed videos. I think this work can lead to promising new directions in SSL research.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
1. In the paper, the authors perform experiments/evaluation solely on videos. I was wondering if the method learns good representations for "image" tasks as well. Namely, leveraging MV can potentially give us better RGB representations for images, too. I wonder what the authors think about this.
2. If the hard-positive mining is based on the learned features, would there be any "chichen-and-egg" problem introduced by the proposed procedure?
3. I like the comparison to MV_{CoViAR}, but I wonder how each specific compression setting affects the results. For example, how does GOP size, the use of B-frame vs P-frame, etc. affect results.

Regarding societal impact, I don't see anything particularly concerning (or more concerning) than other SSL work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces a self-supervised approach for learning strong visual representations directly from compressed video. Unlike most prior SSL work that relies solely on RGB, the method exploits motion cues already present in compressed streams, yielding better efficiency. The core idea is to apply a contrastive objective that uses correspondence across modalities to supervise one with the other. The method is evaluated primarily on UCF and HMDB, and shows solid results in both linear probing and transfer learning settings.

Main Review:
Originality (medium-high)
- Leveraging compressed video for SSL is a fresh and original angle; most existing work on compressed videos has focused on supervised training. While the overall implementation aligns with standard practice, that is appropriate here and not a negative.

Quality (medium-high)
- The paper provides the key ablations one would expect. In particular, the experiments clearly show the benefit of the compressed-video modality (MV) over RGB-only training (Table 3). Relative to prior art, performance is competitive while also being substantially more efficient. The comparisons with optical flow and low-resolution optical flow are also informative (Table 2).

Clarity (high)
- The manuscript is generally very clear. One point needing clarification: in the hard-positive mining stage, what exact similarity measure is used?

Significance (medium-high)
- SSL remains a highly active and impactful area. This work adds a valuable perspective by exploiting compressed video, and it could open promising lines of inquiry for future SSL research.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
1) All experiments are on video. It would be useful to understand whether the learned features transfer to image-only tasks as well; since MV might enhance RGB representations, there could be benefits for images too. What is the authors’ view on this?
2) Since hard-positive mining relies on learned features, does the procedure introduce a chicken-and-egg issue?
3) The comparison to MV_{CoViAR} is appreciated, but the impact of compression specifics remains unclear. How do factors like GOP size and the presence of B- versus P-frames influence outcomes?

Regarding societal impact, there is nothing notably more concerning than for other SSL methodologies.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

Original Decision (Rating)
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors propose a self-supervised learning (SSL) method for learning strong visual representations based on compressed videos. Compared with prior work, which sees videos in RGB only, the proposed method is more efficient and leverages the motion information that's already accessible in compressed videos. The authors propose to use a contrastive learning method to supervise on kind of modality with correspondence of another modality. On multiple datasets (mainly UCF and HMDB), good performance is achieved, with both linear probing and transfer learning settings.

Main Review:
[originality: medium-high]
 - The idea of using compressed videos for self-supervised learning is novel/original. Most prior work that operates on compressed videos focues on supervised learning setting only. The implementation of this idea is standard/expected (this is not a drawback).

[quality: medium-high]
 - The paper covers useful ablation study that I'd expect. In particular, the authors demonstrate the usefulness of the compressed-video modality (MV) compared to RGB only (Table 3). Compared to prior work, the performance is also strong while being significantly more efficient. The ablation study that compares with optical flow and low-res optical flow is also interesting (Table 2).

[clarity: high]
 - I think this paper is overall clearly writen. One question about implementation details: How is the similarity calculated in the hard-positive mining procedure?

[significance: medium-high]
 - The area of SSL draws a lot of attention in our community and is of great potential. I think this work extends our knowledge from a new dimension with the use of compressed videos. I think this work can lead to promising new directions in SSL research.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
1. In the paper, the authors perform experiments/evaluation solely on videos. I was wondering if the method learns good representations for "image" tasks as well. Namely, leveraging MV can potentially give us better RGB representations for images, too. I wonder what the authors think about this.
2. If the hard-positive mining is based on the learned features, would there be any "chichen-and-egg" problem introduced by the proposed procedure?
3. I like the comparison to MV_{CoViAR}, but I wonder how each specific compression setting affects the results. For example, how does GOP size, the use of B-frame vs P-frame, etc. affect results.

Regarding societal impact, I don't see anything particularly concerning (or more concerning) than other SSL work.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: The paper tackles the problem of self-supervised video representation learning. The authors propose a compressed video based self-supervised learning approach, where the model is forced to learn matching representations between raw RGB clip and its corresponding motion vector clip. This is achieved using a relatively standard contrastive learning formulation. The authors then demonstrate that their model achieves state-of-the-art performance on a few downstream tasks (i.e., action recognition, and video retrieval) while also being significantly more efficient (particularly in terms of the video processing time).
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
- The paper is written clearly, and it addresses a well motivated and an important research problem.
- A nice idea of using compressed video for self-supervised feature learning.
- The proposed approach delivers performance that is comparable/better to previous methods (e.g. CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b]) while being much more efficient.
- Thorough ablation studies.

Weaknesses:
- The major weakness of this paper is its limited technical novelty. While the idea of using compressed videos in the context of self-supervised learning is new and interesting, overall the proposed approach is largely built on the ideas from CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b]. Having said this, the proposed approach seems to be both more effective and more efficient than CoCLR. Therefore, I think this would be a good "poster" paper.
- The proposed approach performs quite a bit worse than the recent CVPR 2021 methods such as CVRL, and especially the method of Feichtenhofer et al. "A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning" (CVPR 2021). Having said this, given a very large computational cost of both of these prior methods (i.e. 800 epoch training), I think that it would be unfair to compare the proposed MVCGC approach directly with these methods. The authors should still add the second reference to their related work section, and also to their quantitative comparisons. A discussion on why the direct comparison between these methods is unfair would also be useful.

Questions / Minor Details:
- In L85-88, the authors mention that unlike CoCLR, they construct sample pairs between different views of different clip (i.e., RGB frames of one clip and motion vectors of another clip from the same video). However, the caption of Fig.3 says that the motion vector clip corresponds to the raw RGB clip. Therefore, I was confused, which one is it? Also, if it's the former, could the authors also ablate the latter?
- Typo in Table 3 (it should be Retrieval instead of Retrival). 
- In the third part of Table 4, the authors highlight/bold the numbers corresponding to their approach even though CVRL achieves higher accuracy.
- If the authors could include the linear evaluation on Kinetics-400 (similar to "A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning") that would be great. I think the evaluation on UCF and HMDB is a bit saturated and outdated. Therefore, it would be useful to have additional numbers on Kinetics, which is a bit more challenging dataset.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitations were not discussed.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This work explores self-supervised learning for video by leveraging compressed video signals. The method trains representations by aligning features from a raw RGB clip with those from its corresponding motion vector clip using a standard contrastive learning setup. The authors report state-of-the-art results on downstream tasks such as action recognition and video retrieval, while offering notable gains in efficiency, especially in video preprocessing.

Main Review:
Strengths:
- The manuscript is clearly written and targets a timely, well-motivated problem.
- Using compressed video for self-supervised feature learning is an appealing idea.
- The approach matches or surpasses prior methods like CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b] while being substantially more efficient.
- The ablation studies are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:
- The main limitation is modest technical novelty. Although applying compressed videos in a self-supervised setting is fresh and intriguing, the overall framework closely follows CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b]. That said, the method appears both more effective and more efficient than CoCLR, making it a solid “poster” contribution.
- The method underperforms relative to recent CVPR 2021 approaches such as CVRL and especially Feichtenhofer et al., “A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning.” Given the very high computational budget required by those methods (e.g., 800 training epochs), a direct comparison is arguably unfair. The authors should still include the latter work in related work and in quantitative tables, along with a brief discussion clarifying why head-to-head comparisons are not equitable.

Questions / Minor Details:
- Lines 85–88 state that, unlike CoCLR, pairs are formed across different clips (e.g., RGB from one clip and motion vectors from another clip within the same video), whereas the caption of Fig. 3 indicates the motion vector clip corresponds to the same RGB clip. This is confusing—please clarify which pairing strategy is used. If it is the cross-clip variant, please also ablate the same-clip pairing.
- Table 3 contains a typo (“Retrival” should be “Retrieval”).
- In the third segment of Table 4, the authors bold their numbers even though CVRL achieves higher accuracy.
- Please consider adding linear evaluation on Kinetics-400 (as in “A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning”). UCF/HMDB are somewhat saturated and dated; Kinetics provides a more challenging and informative benchmark.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitations are not discussed.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper presents a self-supervised algorithm for representation learning of videos. The authors propose using RGB clips and Motion Vectors (MV) available in compressed videos to reduce the computational burden of processing. The two streams are seen as views in the contrastive setting: positive pairs are composed of RGB and MV clips of the same video, negative ones have clips belonging to different samples. Moreover, hard positive mining extends the set of positive pairs by seeking other videos that are similar in both views. The approach compares favorably on action datasets UC101 and HMDB51 for both action recognition and video retrieval tasks.
reviews.main_review:
## Strenghts
* The contribution of this paper is timely: the computational requirements of contrastive learning hinders its applicability on video representation learning. Compressed videos enables the shrinkage of required time and storage.
* The work proves how effective use of the less informative MV is on par with the computationally demanding optical flow
* Extensive experiments on action recognition and retrieval prove that the proposed gets competitive results with state of the art methods: linear evaluation and full fine-tuning accuracy for action recognition and R@k metrics for retrieval.

## Weaknesses
* The method builds on [1] by extending the single-view contrastive approach to learn the correspondence between two views and changing the hard-positive condition. However, it is well known that cross-view contrastive is beneficial [2, 3, 4]. Yet, the showed technique for harvesting hard positives is an incremental contribution to the literature.
* How cross guidance and hard positive mining contribute to the final performance is not well disentangled and it's not easy to understand what their impact is. What's the performance loss without hard-positives? What is the effect of using hard positives in the initialization phase? What is the performance of the ensemble of RGB and MV model right after initialization?

### Minor
* Table 2: I find somewhat surprising that the same technique applied to OF gets lower performances, could the authors provide some explanations/insights?
* Line 258: concerning the claim "MVCGC is robust to motion vectors under different encoding configurations": is not really clear how this is proved. In table 2 MV_CoViAr is used for initialization only.
* Line 303: I think that it is "by retrieval with only" RGB "are even higher"
* Appendix, fig 1: the underlying assumptions is that semantically similar videos are close in both RGB and MV space. How do the authors think the model would handle failures of such assumption, e.g., mopping a gymnastic floor, biking in a volleyball arena, etc.?
--------------------
* **Correctness:** The proposed method and the experimental evaluation are sound.
* **Clarity:** The paper is well written and easy to follow. The content is well organized: contribution is expressed concisely and the experimental part is well explained.
* **Relation to prior work:** Authors relates their work with previous literature while concisely highlighting their contribution.
* **Reproducibility:** Architecture, method and optimization hyperparameters are available for implementation, missing augmentation parameters (random cropping, blurring, jittering) and missing code limits the reproducibility of the work though.
* **Originality**: The contribution of this paper is incremental with respect to cited papers, the real advantages of the technique are not thoroughly highlighted in the ablation part.
------------------------
[1] Han, Tengda, Weidi Xie, and Andrew Zisserman. "Self-supervised Co-Training for Video Representation Learning." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 33 (2020): 5679-5690.

[2] Tian, Yonglong, Dilip Krishnan, and Phillip Isola. "Contrastive multiview coding." Computer Vision–ECCV 2020: 16th European Conference, Glasgow, UK, August 23–28, 2020, Proceedings, Part XI 16. Springer International Publishing, 2020.

[3] Bachman, Philip, R. Devon Hjelm, and William Buchwalter. "Learning Representations by Maximizing Mutual Information Across Views." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 32 (2019): 15535-15545.

[4] Chen, Ting, et al. "A simple framework for contrastive learning of visual representations." International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: N/A. However, I would argue that no specific discussion is needed on potential negative societal impact for a contrastive learning method.

* The work proves how effective use of the less informative MV is on par with the computationally demanding optical flow
* Extensive experiments on action recognition and retrieval prove that the proposed gets competitive results with state of the art methods: linear evaluation and full fine-tuning accuracy for action recognition and R@k metrics for retrieval.

## Weaknesses
* The method builds on [1] by extending the single-view contrastive approach to learn the correspondence between two views and changing the hard-positive condition. However, it is well known that cross-view contrastive is beneficial [2, 3, 4]. Yet, the showed technique for harvesting hard positives is an incremental contribution to the literature.
* How cross guidance and hard positive mining contribute to the final performance is not well disentangled and it's not easy to understand what their impact is. What's the performance loss without hard-positives? What is the effect of using hard positives in the initialization phase? * Appendix, fig 1: the underlying assumptions is that semantically similar videos are close in both RGB and MV space. How do the authors think the model would handle failures of such assumption, e.g., mopping a gymnastic floor, biking in a volleyball arena, etc.?

reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper introduces a self-supervised method for learning video representations that leverages RGB clips together with Motion Vectors (MVs) extracted from compressed video to cut computational cost. The two streams are treated as different views in a contrastive framework: positives pair RGB and MV segments from the same video, while negatives come from different videos. The authors also perform hard positive mining by identifying additional videos that are close in both views to expand the positive set. On UCF101 and HMDB51, the approach reports strong results for both action recognition and video retrieval.

Main Review:
Strengths
- The contribution is timely: contrastive learning for videos is often compute- and storage-heavy, and working with compressed video substantially reduces both.
- The paper shows that carefully using the less informative MV modality can rival the performance of optical flow, which is far more computationally expensive.
- The empirical study is broad and compelling, covering action recognition (linear probing and full fine-tuning) and retrieval (R@k), with competitive outcomes relative to the state of the art.

Weaknesses
- The method mainly builds on [1], evolving a single-view contrastive setup into a cross-view one and modifying the hard-positive selection. While cross-view contrastive learning is already established as beneficial [2, 3, 4], the proposed strategy for mining hard positives feels like a modest, incremental step.
- The roles of cross guidance and hard positive mining are not cleanly teased apart. It is hard to assess their individual effects. What is the performance drop without hard positives? How does using hard positives during initialization affect the results? What accuracy does the RGB+MV ensemble achieve immediately after initialization?

Minor
- Table 2: It is surprising that applying the same technique to optical flow yields worse results. Please provide insights or an explanation.
- Line 258: The claim that “MVCGC is robust to motion vectors under different encoding configurations” is not convincingly demonstrated. In Table 2, MV_CoViAr appears to be used only for initialization.
- Line 303: The phrasing likely should be “by retrieval with only RGB are even higher.”
- Appendix, Fig. 1: The assumption seems to be that semantically similar videos are close in both RGB and MV spaces. How would the model behave when this assumption is violated (e.g., mopping a gymnastics floor, biking in a volleyball arena)?

Additional Assessments
- Correctness: The method and experiments appear technically sound.
- Clarity: The paper is clearly written and well structured; the contributions are succinctly stated and the experiments are well described.
- Relation to prior work: The paper situates itself appropriately within the literature and articulates the contribution clearly.
- Reproducibility: The architecture, methodology, and optimization hyperparameters are given, but missing augmentation details (e.g., random crop, blur, color jitter) and the absence of code hinder full reproducibility.
- Originality: The contribution is incremental with respect to the referenced works; the ablation studies do not fully elucidate the method’s unique advantages.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
N/A. Given the nature of the work (contrastive learning methodology), a discussion of potential negative societal impacts is not strictly necessary.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

References:
[1] Han, Tengda, Weidi Xie, and Andrew Zisserman. “Self-supervised Co-Training for Video Representation Learning.” NeurIPS 33 (2020): 5679–5690.
[2] Tian, Yonglong, Dilip Krishnan, and Phillip Isola. “Contrastive Multiview Coding.” ECCV 2020.
[3] Bachman, Philip, R. Devon Hjelm, and William Buchwalter. “Learning Representations by Maximizing Mutual Information Across Views.” NeurIPS 32 (2019): 15535–15545.
[4] Chen, Ting, et al. “A Simple Framework for Contrastive Learning of Visual Representations.” ICML 2020.

title: Unifying Gradient Estimators for Meta-Reinforcement Learning  via Off-Policy Evaluation

reviews.summary:
The paper uses the recently developed framework of Taylor expansions for value functions (ref [29] in the paper), expands the results, and shows how they could be applied to Off-Policy Evaluation, and by a non-trivial extension to meta-learning. The main algorthmic contribution of the paper is the application of the ideas to meta-learning, but I feel the main contribution is a conceptual one, further developing the ideas from [29] and drawing theoretical connections between OPE and meta-learning.

The paper includes an experimental section demonstrating marginal improvement over baselines in several domains.
reviews.main_review:
Overview: Despite some flaws, I think this is a good paper and leaning towards acceptance. I think the two main limitations of the paper are:
(1) It draws very heavily on the theory and novelty of [29] and therefore its own novelty is a bit limited.
(2) The empirical improvements obtained for meta-learning (the problem the paper sets out to advance) are limited.
Additionally, there are some changes that should be made to make the paper clearer, but I think those can be easily fixed for the camera ready if the paper is accepted. Despite the drawbacks, the reason I support acceptance is that I feel the additional contributions on top of the already interesting results of [29] are non-trivial, and this is a potentially interesting research direction which could provide insight into many aspects of RL. For example, viewing meta-learning through the lens of Taylor expansions for value functions allows for a very clear connection and unification of derivatives estimations of value functions and OPE.

A general suggestion for the authors which I feel might improve the overall story of the paper, is to lean more heavily on the theoretical results and their ability to shed light on interesting insights such as the connection between OPE and meta-learning, and OPE to high order derivatives. At least my personal experience reading the paper was expecting significant novelty and improvement of results on meta-learning, which set me up for a disappointment. It was only on a second read that I appreciated the theoretical insights in the first half of the paper more. Additionally, I would set up the connection with [29] more - the authors do a good enough job of properly citing [29], but not enough to make it clear that the reader should be familiar with [29] to fully appreciate this paper. After emphasizing the theory and insights, I think the meta-learning algorithm and result can be presented as a cool implication (this is obviously a very subjective suggestion).

Some more specific comments:
- In lines 120-122 regarding how the approximation maintains the higher-order dependencies on \theta. I feel like I'm missing something and the sentence is failing to convey an idea it is trying to. Obviously when you make an approximation in some parameter you maintain higher order terms in the small parameter, and choose how many terms to keep. So this statement seems trivial and I'd like to know if there's something deeper which the authors are trying to convey and I'm missing.
- In section 3.2 and Proposition 3.1 - Is K a typo and should be m? (if yes, it is a consistent typo, which is why I'm confused). If it isn't a typo, I couldn't see where K is defined, and also can't follow the proof of Proportion 3.1, so this is a point I would really like clarification on.
- In section 5.1, the authors claim to investigate the bias/variance tradeoff of their method, but only show results for the overall MSE of estimating the derivative. I think the authors should show explicit plots of the decomposed variance and bias of each estimator shown.
- very minor - the y-labels in Figure 2 a-b are cut.

The authors do a good job of discussing the limitations of their method compared to alternative methods.

The paper is mostly theoretical and I don't see a potential negative soceital impact.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This work builds on the recent framework of Taylor expansions for value functions (paper’s ref. [29]), extends those results, and demonstrates applications to Off-Policy Evaluation, with a nontrivial extension to meta-learning. While the primary algorithmic novelty lies in the meta-learning application, I see the main contribution as conceptual: deepening the theory from [29] and establishing theoretical links between OPE and meta-learning. The experiments show only modest gains over baselines across several domains.

Main Review:
Overall assessment: Despite some issues, I consider this a solid submission and I lean toward acceptance. The two most significant weaknesses are:
(1) The paper relies substantially on the theory and novelty introduced in [29], which limits its standalone originality.
(2) The empirical improvements for meta-learning—the focal problem of the paper—are modest.

There are also clarity issues that should be addressed, but these seem straightforward to resolve in the camera-ready version if accepted. I still favor acceptance because the added contributions beyond [29] are nontrivial and the direction is promising for RL. Framing meta-learning through Taylor expansions of value functions provides a clean unification of derivative estimation for value functions and OPE.

A broader recommendation: consider centering the narrative more strongly on the theoretical results and their implications—for instance, the connections between OPE and meta-learning and the link from OPE to higher-order derivatives. My initial read left me expecting substantial novelty and stronger meta-learning results, which was disappointing; on a second pass, I appreciated the theoretical insights much more. It would also help to foreground the relationship to [29] more explicitly. Although [29] is cited appropriately, it is not obvious that familiarity with [29] is essential to fully grasp this paper. With the theory highlighted, the meta-learning algorithm and results could be framed as a compelling downstream consequence (admittedly a subjective suggestion).

Specific comments:
- Lines 120–122: The statement about preserving higher-order dependencies on θ feels tautological—approximations typically retain higher-order terms in the small parameter as chosen. If there is a subtler point intended, it does not come through; please clarify.
- Section 3.2 and Proposition 3.1: Is K a typo for m? If it is, it’s used consistently, which adds to the confusion; if not, I don’t see where K is defined. As is, I’m unable to follow the proof of Proposition 3.1 and would appreciate clarification.
- Section 5.1: You claim to study the bias–variance trade-off, but only report MSE for derivative estimation. Please include separate plots for bias and variance for each estimator.
- Minor: The y-axis labels in Figure 2a–b are truncated.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The discussion of limitations relative to alternative approaches is well done. The paper is largely theoretical, and I do not foresee negative societal impacts.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This paper considers meta-RL with gradient-based adaptations, which relies heavily on the estimation of the Hessians of value functions. Though there are previous approaches that focus on unbiased/biased estimations of Hessian. This paper gives a unified view for estimating higher-order derivatives of value functions, through the lens of off-policy evaluation. This subsumes several previous works, also sheds some light on the bias-variance tradeoff of the estimate. Empirically, the new proposed second-order estimate is also incorporated in meta-RL algorithms to showcase the empirical efficiency.
reviews.main_review:
(Originality): The main contributions I see from this paper are: (1). a unified framework for estimating higher-order derivatives of value functions, through OPE methods. (2). the use of Taylor expansion in the value function, which controls the number of IS ratios in the value estimate, hence sheds some light on the bias-variance trade-off. (3). also a new method arising from this framework.

(Quality): The motivation is clear, and the method is clearly stated. Some comments here include:
[1]. For the bias-variance trade-off with the Taylor expansion, how to select the optimal $K$? Also, how does this bias-variance trade-off translate into the downstream meta-gradient estimations? Any comments on this?
[2]. In experiment section 5.1, the bias-variance trade-off examination seems a little bit confusing to me. It seems the argument here is the second-order method achieves a better trade-off through high correlation with the ground truth. Is there any way to empirically examine the bias and variance in this tabular setting?
[3]. For the continuous control problem, it seems the improvement from the new proposed approach is pretty marginal since the bias might be a dominating issue here. Are there any environments/MDPs/settings that we can expect a larger improvement of it? It would be great to include some discussions/new experiments on this.

(Clarity): The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitations are discussed in Section 3, while it seems it does not have any societal impact.

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper studies meta-RL with gradient-based adaptation, where accurate estimation of Hessians of value functions is crucial. Building on prior work on both unbiased and biased Hessian estimators, the authors present a unified perspective for computing higher-order derivatives of value functions using off-policy evaluation. This framework encompasses several existing methods and clarifies the inherent bias-variance trade-offs. Empirically, they integrate a newly derived second-order estimator into meta-RL algorithms and demonstrate practical efficiency.

Main Review:
Originality:
- The key contributions appear to be: (1) a unified OPE-based framework for estimating higher-order value function derivatives; (2) leveraging a Taylor expansion of the value function to control the number of importance sampling ratios, thereby illuminating the bias-variance trade-off; and (3) a new estimator that naturally emerges from this framework.

Quality:
- The motivation is sound and the approach is described clearly. I have several questions/comments:
  [1] Regarding the Taylor truncation and bias-variance trade-off, how should one choose the optimal K in practice? Moreover, how does this trade-off propagate to the final meta-gradient estimates used downstream? Any guidance or analysis here would be helpful.
  [2] The analysis of bias-variance in Section 5.1 is somewhat unclear. The claim seems to be that the second-order method strikes a better balance due to its higher correlation with ground truth. In the tabular case, is there a way to directly measure and report empirical bias and variance rather than relying on correlation alone?
  [3] In continuous control, the gains from the proposed method are fairly modest, likely because bias dominates in these settings. Are there specific environments/MDPs where larger improvements are expected? Additional discussion or experiments exploring such settings would strengthen the case.

Clarity:
- The manuscript is well-written and easy to follow.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
- Limitations are outlined in Section 3. There appears to be no societal impact.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Decision:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper is concerned with meta reinforcement learning. The author(s) introduced a framework for learning high-order derivatives of value functions using off-policy evaluation. Specifically, the author(s) proposed to adopt existing off-policy evaluation methods to derive the value estimator and then take the derivative with respect to the target parameters to construct the estimate. This technique is further adopted to implement meta reinforcement learning.
reviews.main_review:
I have a few major comments regarding the proposed procedure.

1. The author(s) proposed to adopt importance sampling and doubly-robust estimators to evaluate the value function. These estimators suffer from the curse of horizon, leading to the sub-optimality of the learned optimal policy. Given that the author(s) considered an MDP setting, there are more efficient estimators for value evaluation (see, e.g., https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.12429; https://arxiv.org/abs/1909.05850).

2. Instead of constructing estimators for the value, it might be more efficient to directly evaluate the gradient of the value and its Hessian matrix. In the single agent setting, there are efficient off-policy policy gradient estimators developed in the literature (see, e.g., https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.04014).

3. I think the author(s) focused on offline meta reinforcement learning problems. It remains unclear to me how the proposed method would work when the positivity assumption (e.g., the policy ratio shall be bounded away from infinity) in the offline dataset is violated. In the single agent setting, the pessimism principle is commonly adopted to mitigate this issue. Would it be useful to combine the pessimism principle with the proposed method?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors have discussed the limitations of their work on Page 6.

Specifically, the author(s) proposed to adopt existing off-policy evaluation methods to derive the value estimator and then take the derivative with respect to the target parameters to construct the estimate. This technique is further adopted to implement meta reinforcement learning.

Main Review:
I have a few major comments regarding the proposed procedure.

1. The author(s) proposed to adopt importance sampling and doubly-robust estimators to evaluate the value function. 3. I think the author(s) focused on offline meta reinforcement learning problems. reviews.text:
Summary:
This work tackles meta reinforcement learning by introducing a framework to compute higher-order derivatives of value functions via off-policy evaluation. The authors first obtain value estimates using existing off-policy evaluation tools, then differentiate these estimates with respect to the target parameters to produce higher-order derivatives. They then leverage this mechanism to implement meta-RL.

Main Review:
I have several principal concerns about the proposed approach:

1) The method relies on importance sampling and doubly robust estimators for value estimation. These are known to suffer from the curse of horizon, which can lead to suboptimal learned policies. Since the setting is an MDP, there exist more sample-efficient estimators for value evaluation that might alleviate these issues (see, e.g., https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.12429; https://arxiv.org/abs/1909.05850).

2) Rather than estimating the value and then differentiating, it may be more effective to directly estimate the value gradient and Hessian. In the single-agent case, there are off-policy policy gradient estimators that are more efficient for this purpose (see, e.g., https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.04014).

3) The paper appears to target offline meta-RL. It is unclear how the method behaves when the positivity condition is violated in the offline data (e.g., policy ratios not bounded). In single-agent offline RL, pessimism is a common strategy to address this; would integrating a pessimistic principle into the proposed framework help?

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The authors outline the limitations of their method on Page 6.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Decision:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
