title: Reinforcement learning for instance segmentation with high-level priors

reviews.summary: This paper presents a strategy for object instance segmentation from images using training data that has no manual annotations. Instead of manual annotations, as is required for many state-of-the-art methods, this paper proposes to use high-level priors represented as rules that can be evaluated in the produced segmentation. These rules define what an acceptable segmentation is, and are used to create a reward signal for a reinforcement learning agent. The framework formulates an environment, an agent, an action space and a reward system in a stateless system to guide segmentation. All the components of the system are well designed and well thought, and this approach may have wide applications in bioimage segmentation problems.
reviews.main_review:
* The stateless actor-critic model has connections with the dynamics of Generative Adversarial Networks, as the authors note. The fact that there is no state also begs the question of what are the connections with the REINFORCE rule for training models when the loss function is non-differentiable, and how that approach could simplify or enhance the proposed formulation.
* The experimental design is interesting and overall complete. However, the performance metrics used for evaluation are not in line with research in the instance segmentation field, where precision and recall are used in several different ways. For example, computing precision or F1 score at specific intersection over union thresholds would be more informative to understand how many objects / regions are correctly segmented and to what level of overlap with ground truth.
* There are a few datasets studied in the instance segmentation literature that could have been used as benchmarks in this evaluation. At least one dataset used for nucleus segmentation of cells, for instance, would add a lot of value to the paper for two reasons: first, it connects with previous literature in the topic, second, it allows to assess how far a model trained without ground truth data would go for segmenting these objects. It is not expected that the proposed model should be better than fully supervised approaches, but being the first in the unsupervised regime would help understand how far we are from a competitive model while not requiring ground truth data.
* The paper is overall well written and clear.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: No comments about limitations. Positive societal impacts expected, as less annotations would be required from experts to obtain accurate models.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a strategy for object instance segmentation from images using training data that has no manual annotations. Instead of manual annotations, as is required for many state-of-the-art methods, this paper proposes to use high-level priors represented as rules that can be evaluated in the produced segmentation. These rules define what an acceptable segmentation is, and are used to create a reward signal for a reinforcement learning agent. The framework formulates an environment, an agent, an action space and a reward system in a stateless system to guide segmentation. All the components of the system are well designed and well thought, and this approach may have wide applications in bioimage segmentation problems.

Main Review:
* The stateless actor-critic model has connections with the dynamics of Generative Adversarial Networks, as the authors note. The fact that there is no state also begs the question of what are the connections with the REINFORCE rule for training models when the loss function is non-differentiable, and how that approach could simplify or enhance the proposed formulation.
* The experimental design is interesting and overall complete. However, the performance metrics used for evaluation are not in line with research in the instance segmentation field, where precision and recall are used in several different ways. For example, computing precision or F1 score at specific intersection over union thresholds would be more informative to understand how many objects / regions are correctly segmented and to what level of overlap with ground truth.
* There are a few datasets studied in the instance segmentation literature that could have been used as benchmarks in this evaluation. At least one dataset used for nucleus segmentation of cells, for instance, would add a lot of value to the paper for two reasons: first, it connects with previous literature in the topic, second, it allows to assess how far a model trained without ground truth data would go for segmenting these objects. It is not expected that the proposed model should be better than fully supervised approaches, but being the first in the unsupervised regime would help understand how far we are from a competitive model while not requiring ground truth data.
* The paper is overall well written and clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary:
This work introduces a method for instance segmentation that dispenses with manual annotations. Instead, it encodes high-level priors as evaluable rules that define acceptable segmentations and convert them into a reward for a reinforcement learning agent. The authors instantiate an environment, agent, action space, and reward function in a stateless setup to drive segmentation. The components are carefully designed and thoughtfully integrated, and the approach appears broadly applicable, particularly to bioimage segmentation.

Main Review:
- The stateless actor-critic framework bears resemblance to GAN dynamics, as the authors acknowledge. The absence of state also raises a natural connection to REINFORCE for handling non-differentiable objectives; clarifying how REINFORCE might relate to, simplify, or strengthen this formulation would be valuable.
- The experiments are well conceived and fairly comprehensive. That said, the evaluation metrics are not aligned with common practice in instance segmentation, where nuanced precision/recall analyses are standard. Reporting precision or F1 at specific IoU thresholds would better convey the proportion of correctly detected instances and the quality of overlap with ground truth.
- There exist established instance segmentation datasets that could serve as benchmarks here. Including at least one nucleus segmentation dataset would significantly strengthen the paper: it would tie this work to prior literature and quantify how far an annotation-free approach can go on a well-studied problem. While superiority over fully supervised methods is not anticipated, establishing a baseline in the unsupervised regime would clarify the performance gap without requiring labeled data.
- The manuscript is generally clear and well written.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
No explicit limitations are discussed. The expected societal impact is positive, as the method reduces reliance on expert annotations to train accurate models.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

Original Decision (Rating)
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a strategy for object instance segmentation from images using training data that has no manual annotations. Instead of manual annotations, as is required for many state-of-the-art methods, this paper proposes to use high-level priors represented as rules that can be evaluated in the produced segmentation. These rules define what an acceptable segmentation is, and are used to create a reward signal for a reinforcement learning agent. The framework formulates an environment, an agent, an action space and a reward system in a stateless system to guide segmentation. All the components of the system are well designed and well thought, and this approach may have wide applications in bioimage segmentation problems.

Main Review:
* The stateless actor-critic model has connections with the dynamics of Generative Adversarial Networks, as the authors note. The fact that there is no state also begs the question of what are the connections with the REINFORCE rule for training models when the loss function is non-differentiable, and how that approach could simplify or enhance the proposed formulation.
* The experimental design is interesting and overall complete. However, the performance metrics used for evaluation are not in line with research in the instance segmentation field, where precision and recall are used in several different ways. For example, computing precision or F1 score at specific intersection over union thresholds would be more informative to understand how many objects / regions are correctly segmented and to what level of overlap with ground truth.
* There are a few datasets studied in the instance segmentation literature that could have been used as benchmarks in this evaluation. At least one dataset used for nucleus segmentation of cells, for instance, would add a lot of value to the paper for two reasons: first, it connects with previous literature in the topic, second, it allows to assess how far a model trained without ground truth data would go for segmenting these objects. It is not expected that the proposed model should be better than fully supervised approaches, but being the first in the unsupervised regime would help understand how far we are from a competitive model while not requiring ground truth data.
* The paper is overall well written and clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No comments about limitations. Positive societal impacts expected, as less annotations would be required from experts to obtain accurate models.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.main_review:
This paper presents a novel idea of applying RL methodology to instance segmentation. The stateless actor-critic reinforcement learning theme enables non-differential rewards which could incorporate prior knowledge to guide the segmentation, and relaxes the heavy requirement of pixel-wise labeling. The idea is unique and quite interesting.
The related work looks sufficient to me. The experiments are well designed with convincing results.
The writing is overall clear, but there are a few parts which could be improved:

2. There is a lot of valuable information in Appendix. It'd be nice to figure out a way to shorten some parts of Method section (such as Section 3.2 and experiments) and include some content from Appendix here.
3. For the experiments, some failure cases and analysis are necessary. It'd be good to add a section of ablation studies.

reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper proposes an instance segmentation framework cast as a graph partitioning problem, where an actor-critic RL model predicts edge weights. Rewards are defined via how well the resulting segments align with high-level priors on object shape, location, and scale.

Main Review:
The use of reinforcement learning for instance segmentation is an original and appealing direction. The stateless actor-critic setup allows the use of non-differentiable reward functions, making it possible to encode prior knowledge to steer the segmentation process while reducing dependence on dense pixel-level annotations. The concept is distinctive and compelling. The related work coverage appears adequate, and the experimental design is solid, with results that are persuasive.

The paper is generally well written, but several aspects could be clarified or reorganized:
1) In Section 3, the key distinction between Method 1 and Method 2 is not sufficiently clear. Including a figure in the main text (akin to Figure 1) to illustrate Method 2—rather than relegating it to the appendix—would help.
2) The appendix contains a substantial amount of useful detail. Consider condensing parts of the Methods (e.g., Section 3.2) and the experiments to bring some of that appendix content into the main paper.
3) The experimental section would benefit from explicit failure cases and accompanying analysis. An ablation study section is also needed.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors proposed a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for instance segmentation with superpixels. The proposed framework is based on a stateless actor-critic setup and is demonstrated to be effective on the instance segmentation tasks for one toy dataset and one microscopy dataset.
reviews.main_review:
+: strength
-: weakness 

1)Originality: 

+: This paper proposes to solve the instance segmentation tasks with weak supervision (superpixels) using the RL based methods. Different from the typical weakly supervised image instance segmentation methods, the RL-based one is a new and interesting application.

-: The authors only talked about the related work on RL however ignore the related work on instance segmentation (fully supervised and weakly supervised). 

2) Quality:

+: The proposed modules are proved to be effective via the ablation studies on the microscopy dataset. In addition, the authors also provide sufficient analysis on the selection of the hyper-parameters, such as the number of feature channels, random seed, etc.

-: There lack extensive experiments on public instance segmentation benchmark datasets, such as MS COCO [a]. In addition, there are also many instance segmentation tasks in microscopy image analysis, such as nuclei instance segmentation in histopathology images [b], cell instance segmentation in electron microscopy images [1].

[a] https://cocodataset.org/#home
[b] A Multi-Organ Nucleus Segmentation Challenge, in IEEE TMI
[1]: the reference #1 in the manuscript.

3) Clarity:

+: The methods and experiment sections are clearly presented.

-: This paper aims at solving the weakly supervised instance segmentation via RL architecture, however, there lack illustrations and discussion on any related instance segmentation works.

4) Significance:

-: Although the authors have presented many experimental results, there still lack experimental results on representative benchmark instance segmentation datasets on general and medical images, which makes me feel that the overall paper lacks significance.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
1) The major concern of this work is the lack of experimental results on public benchmark datasets. For the first toy dataset, the background content seems to be fixed (black content and white ‘noise’). For the second microscopy dataset, the morphological structure of the instances is always special: all the instances overlapping together to form a circle-like fruitfly embryo. To this end, the overall experiments on the two datasets are not sufficient to demonstrate the proposed method can be effective on other general datasets, such as the MS COCO. This made the overall impact of the proposed method questionable.  

2) It seems that there are only 10 testing images in the real microscopy dataset, which is a small number. Therefore, a statistical significance test (based on p-value) is suggested for the results on this dataset.

3) Computational complexity analysis is also suggested on the proposed framework.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces a reinforcement learning framework for instance segmentation using superpixels, built around a stateless actor–critic design. The approach is evaluated on a synthetic toy dataset and a microscopy dataset, where it shows promising performance.

Main Review:
+: strengths
-: weaknesses

1) Originality:

+ The work explores weakly supervised instance segmentation from superpixels via an RL paradigm. Compared with conventional weakly supervised instance segmentation techniques, applying RL here is novel and intriguing.

- The discussion of related work focuses almost exclusively on RL, with little to no coverage of the broader instance segmentation literature (both fully and weakly supervised).

2) Quality:

+ The component contributions are supported by ablation studies on the microscopy dataset. The paper also includes useful analyses of hyperparameter choices, such as the number of feature channels and random seed variability.

- The experimental scope is limited: key public benchmarks are missing, notably MS COCO [a]. Moreover, the paper overlooks other relevant instance segmentation problems in microscopy, e.g., nuclei segmentation in histopathology [b] and cell instance segmentation in electron microscopy [1].

[a] https://cocodataset.org/#home
[b] A Multi-Organ Nucleus Segmentation Challenge, in IEEE TMI
[1]: the reference #1 in the manuscript.

3) Clarity:

+ The method and experimental setup are described in a clear and accessible manner.

- While the paper targets weakly supervised instance segmentation via an RL framework, it provides minimal figures and discussion situating the method within existing instance segmentation research.

4) Significance:

- Despite the breadth of reported experiments, the absence of results on representative instance segmentation benchmarks across both general and medical domains limits the overall significance of the contribution.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
1) The central concern is the lack of evaluation on well-established public benchmarks. The toy dataset appears to have a highly constrained background (black with white “noise”), and the microscopy dataset features a very specific morphology where overlapping instances form a circle-like fruit fly embryo. These two settings are insufficient to demonstrate applicability to more general datasets such as MS COCO, raising questions about the broader impact of the approach.

2) The real microscopy dataset seems to include only 10 test images, which is quite small. A statistical significance analysis (e.g., using p-values) is recommended for the reported comparisons.

3) An analysis of the computational complexity of the framework is also recommended.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

Original Decision (Rating)
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper proposes the use of RL for instance segmentation. In particular, the paper uses super pixels together with graph partitioning and RL to learn the labels of instances. The model is validated on two datasets, one synthetic and one biomedical imaging dataset and is numerically compared to two approaches ([28] and [47]).
reviews.main_review:
**Originality:**

In the paper, the main motivation of the use of RL is to enable optimization of discrete objective. However, this use of RL is standard. Moreover, given this paper motivations, the reviewer would expect to see empirical comparisons to alternative methods to learn discrete assignments  (e.g. straight through, DETR-style https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.12872, [8]). Thus, from the methodological point of view the novelty is rather limited. From the application point of view, the instance segmentation of light microscopy images, the paper might have some edge of originality; however, it is hard to assess it given the niche domain. To fully appreciate the originality of the method it would be beneficial to validate the approach on a more standard benchmark. 


**Clarity:**

The paper is generally clearly written. However, the clarity of the methods could be improved. The presentation of this section is a bit convoluted making it hard to grasp the overall picture of the proposed approach. The reviewer would encourage the authors to simplify the presentation of the Methods, especially focusing on the aspects of why particular decisions were made. E.g. why are the authors considering method 1 and 2? Why stateless RL with sequence length of 1? Why not use straight through?

For a non-microscopy expert, it is hard to understand the color codings of Figs 4 and 5. 


**Quality and significance:**

The quality and significance of the results are hard to assess given the niche application domain. It would be beneficial to test the ideas on some more standard benchmarks to instance image segmentation (e.g. COCO). 

Additional questions comments:
- Why are superpixels a good prior for this task? 
- Would it be possible to use other priors/partitioning methods?
- It would be nice to discuss the tradeoffs between added computational costs due to the use of RL and metric gains.
- Would it be possible to report the numbers of a standard instance segmentation model for this task, (e.g. https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.06870 or some followup)?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: There is no limitation section/paragraph nor societal impact section/paragraph. Please add those to the conclusions section.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

Summary:
The paper proposes the use of RL for instance segmentation. In particular, the paper uses super pixels together with graph partitioning and RL to learn the labels of instances. The model is validated on two datasets, one synthetic and one biomedical imaging dataset and is numerically compared to two approaches ([28] and [47]).

Main Review:
**Originality:**

In the paper, the main motivation of the use of RL is to enable optimization of discrete objective. However, this use of RL is standard. Moreover, given this paper motivations, the reviewer would expect to see empirical comparisons to alternative methods to learn discrete assignments  (e.g. straight through, DETR-style https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.12872, [8]). Thus, from the methodological point of view the novelty is rather limited. From the application point of view, the instance segmentation of light microscopy images, the paper might have some edge of originality; however, it is hard to assess it given the niche domain. The reviewer would encourage the authors to simplify the presentation of the Methods, especially focusing on the aspects of why particular decisions were made. E.g. why are the authors considering method 1 and 2? Why stateless RL with sequence length of 1? Why not use straight through?

For a non-microscopy expert, it is hard to understand the color codings of Figs 4 and 5. 


**Quality and significance:**

The quality and significance of the results are hard to assess given the niche application domain. It would be beneficial to test the ideas on some more standard benchmarks to instance image segmentation (e.g. COCO). 

Additional questions comments:
- Why are superpixels a good prior for this task? 
- Would it be possible to use other priors/partitioning methods?
- It would be nice to discuss the tradeoffs between added computational costs due to the use of RL and metric gains.
- Would it be possible to report the numbers of a standard instance segmentation model for this task, (e.g. https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.06870 or some followup)?

Limitations And Societal Impact:

4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary:
This work explores reinforcement learning for instance segmentation. The approach combines superpixels, graph partitioning, and RL to infer instance labels. The method is evaluated on two datasets—a synthetic set and a biomedical imaging dataset—and is compared numerically against two baselines ([28] and [47]).

Main Review:

Originality:
The central rationale for adopting RL is to handle optimization over discrete variables. However, this rationale and its implementation are fairly conventional. Given this framing, I would have expected empirical comparisons with other strategies for learning discrete assignments (e.g., straight-through estimators, DETR-style mechanisms https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.12872, [8]). As a result, the methodological novelty appears limited. From an application standpoint, focusing on instance segmentation in light microscopy may offer some originality, but it is difficult to gauge given the specialized nature of the domain. Demonstrating performance on a more widely used benchmark would help clarify the contribution’s originality.

Clarity:
The manuscript is mostly readable, but the methodological description could be clearer. The Methods section is somewhat tangled, which obscures the high-level structure of the approach. I recommend streamlining this section, with particular emphasis on the rationale behind design choices. For instance: Why are both “method 1” and “method 2” considered? Why opt for stateless RL with sequence length 1? Why not employ a straight-through estimator? Additionally, for readers outside microscopy, the color schemes in Figures 4 and 5 are difficult to interpret.

Quality and significance:
Because the application area is quite specialized, it is challenging to judge the broader impact and significance of the results. Evaluating the approach on a standard instance segmentation benchmark (e.g., COCO) would strengthen the assessment of quality and relevance.

Additional questions and comments:
- What makes superpixels a suitable prior for this problem?
- Could alternative priors or partitioning strategies be used?
- Please discuss the trade-offs between the additional computational overhead introduced by RL and the observed performance gains.
- Can you report results for a standard instance segmentation model on this task (e.g., Mask R-CNN https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.06870 or a successor) for comparison?

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The paper lacks explicit sections on limitations and societal impact. Please include these in the conclusions.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

Decision:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: Finite-Time Error Bounds for Distributed Linear Stochastic Approximation

reviews.summary: An interesting submission dealing with distributed stochastic approximation driven by Markovian noise, in which the communication topologies considered among agents are captured by a stochastic matrix (in contrast to the doubly stochastic matrix studied in the related literature). Both asymptotic and finite-time error bounds are established and it was shown that the algorithm converges to some unspecified convex combination of the equilibrium points of local tasks. This paper finally proposes a push-type distributed stochastic approximation algorithm and provides its finite-time bounds for the performance by leveraging the analysis for the consensus-type algorithm with stochastic matrices. All analysis and error bounds are directly applicable to distributed TD algorithms.
reviews.main_review:
Originality: This paper studies distributed Markovian stochastic approximation algorithms with stochastic interconnection matrices, which has not yet been covered in the related literature. The results are a bit different than what is known for related algorithms having doubly stochastic interconnection matrices. Further, a push-type algorithm is proposed for decentralized implementation. 

Quality. The results are technically sound and theoretically supported. The algorithm proposed is also new.

Clarity. The paper is well written and easy to follow. The results are nicely organized and the contributions relative to existing works are clear too.

Significance. The results are important and complement the existing distributed Markovian stochastic approximation results which have only focused on doubly stochastic interconnections among agents. The tools developed for analysis may also be useful for further use.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Numerical simulations may be provided comparing the proposed algorithm and existing ones as well as validating the proved finite-time error bounds for both constant/time-varying stepsizes.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This manuscript examines distributed stochastic approximation under Markovian noise when agent interactions are governed by a column- or row-stochastic mixing matrix rather than the doubly stochastic matrices typically used in prior work. The authors establish both asymptotic guarantees and finite-time error bounds, showing that the iterates converge to an unspecified convex combination of the local equilibrium points. Building on these insights, they introduce a push-style distributed stochastic approximation method and derive finite-time performance bounds for it by leveraging the analysis developed for a consensus-type scheme with stochastic weights. The theoretical framework and bounds directly extend to distributed temporal-difference learning.

Main Review:
Originality. The paper addresses distributed Markovian stochastic approximation with merely stochastic (not doubly stochastic) interconnection matrices—a setting not treated in the existing literature. The resulting properties differ in meaningful ways from the well-studied doubly stochastic case. In addition, the paper proposes a push-based algorithm suitable for decentralized execution.

Quality. The theoretical development is solid and rigorous, and the new algorithm is original.

Clarity. The exposition is clear and well structured, making the technical contributions easy to follow. The relationship to prior work and the paper’s specific advances are articulated cleanly.

Significance. The findings are valuable and broaden the scope of distributed Markovian stochastic approximation beyond doubly stochastic networks. The analytical tools introduced here are likely to be of independent interest for future studies.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
Including numerical experiments would strengthen the paper—both to compare the proposed method with existing approaches and to empirically verify the finite-time error bounds for constant and time-varying step sizes.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

Original Decision (Rating)
7: Good paper, accept

All analysis and error bounds are directly applicable to distributed TD algorithms.

The results are a bit different than what is known for related algorithms having doubly stochastic interconnection matrices. reviews.summary: This paper studies a distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm. The interconnection structure among the agents is described by a time varying directed graph. Under the setting of Markovian samples, the main contribution is to characterize the finite time error bound on the mean squared error. The analysis is applied to study a push-sum version of distributed linear stochastic approximation.
reviews.main_review:
As mentioned, the main contribution is a new analysis on the distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm given in (1), encompassing constant stepsize and time varying step size, Markovian samples. To achieve this, the study introduces a technique known as the absolute probability sequence to track the evolution of error in light of the time varying directed graph which results in a row-stochastic interconnection matrix. This technique can be interesting as it leads to a simple recursion of the iterates in (4), simplifying the subsequent analysis.

However, there are a number of drawbacks which outweigh the benefits of the current paper, as discussed below:

- Though the paper title reads "distributed linear stochastic approximation", the algorithm in (1) only considers a restricted setting with an identical driving matrix $A(x)$ across agents (and different $b^i(x)$ across agents). This setting may be suitable for the distributed policy evaluation problem where a number of prior works have appeared, but it does not include many interesting cases for distributed linear stochastic approximation including classical ones such as linear regression. In this regard, the paper may better position itself as a paper for distributed policy evaluation insteado of the more general linear stochastic approximation. 

- The main theorems (Theorem 2 & 3) require Assumption 6 which is not well justified. Particularly, this assumption requires the absolute probability seqeunce to admit a limit vector. The Remark 3 which follows the assumption fails to provide a convincing case for this assumption to hold. Therein, two examples are given: the case $W_t$ is doubly stochastic is rather trivial as it defies one of the main relaxation considered in this paper; the case when the number of neighbors of agents are unchanged over time (also, is it the number of out-neighbors? or in-neighbors?) appears to be too artificial. The paper shall include a more concrete construction on how the assumption holds. 

- In addition, it is not clear what are the benefits of employing the absolute probability sequence technique in analyzing the convergence of distributed algorithms, e.g., when compared to the classical approach in [52] for the push-SA algorithm. As for the convergence to a weighted average of the equilibrium point when a row-stochastic mixing matrix is employed, some relevant work can be found in the static graph setting, e.g.,

-- J. Chen, A. Sayed, "Distributed Pareto Optimization via Diffusion Strategies", IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing, 2013. 

- The presentation of the main theorems lacks useful information that a finite-time error analysis should encompass. For instance, it is mentioned that "$C_2$ depends on $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$ ..." which does not describe how small/large the bound is - in fact, without a more detailed description, the bound in (7) is not useful since $C_2$ can be large; the result for push-SA is summarized in Theorem 5 *only* for the case of time varying step size, and it is mentioned that "our current analysis approach cannot be directly applied for this case" which lacks further expatiation; in theorem 3, how does that the convergence rate of $\eta_t$ affects the finite time error bound in the constant step size case? 

---
**Post Rebuttal**

The reviewer appreciates the detailed response from the authors. Unfortunately, the response has not addressed my previous concerns and therefore my previous evaluation stands. In particular, the promised "simulation based" study will not be very convincing since cases which violate Assumption 6 may still include a lot of cases. Instead, it would be better to construct an **analytical** example to illustrate the necessity of Assumption 6. In summary, my main concern is that Assumption 6 and its corresponding analysis due to the "absolute probability sequence" is one of the main innovations claimed in the paper, yet its applications are so far only limited to the previously known cases. 

Moreover, while it is acceptable that $C_2$ can be a large constant, it is also important to point out **how** does $C_2$ depends on the system parameter $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$. The current presentation has so far eluded from discussing about that.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: There isn't any potential negative societal impact. The limitation/suggestions for improvement have been discussed in the main review above.

reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper analyzes a distributed linear stochastic approximation scheme over time-varying directed networks with Markovian data. Its primary contribution is a finite-time mean-squared error bound, and the methodology is further specialized to a push-sum variant of distributed linear SA.

Main Review:
The key technical contribution is a new analysis of the algorithm in (1), covering both constant and time-varying stepsizes with Markovian samples. To handle the time-varying, row-stochastic mixing induced by directed graphs, the paper employs the absolute probability sequence to track error evolution. This idea is potentially appealing because it yields the concise recursion in (4), which streamlines parts of the proof.

That said, several issues currently outweigh the advantages:

- Scope mismatch: Despite the title “distributed linear stochastic approximation,” the algorithm in (1) works under the restrictive condition that all agents share the same driving matrix A(x) (while b^i(x) can differ). This setup aligns with distributed policy evaluation—an area with existing literature—but excludes many standard distributed LSA problems, including canonical cases like linear regression. The paper would be more accurately positioned as addressing distributed policy evaluation, rather than general distributed LSA.

- Assumption 6 is insufficiently justified: The main results (Theorems 2 and 3) hinge on Assumption 6, which requires the absolute probability sequence to converge to a limit vector. Remark 3 does not convincingly motivate when this happens. Of the two examples provided: (i) the doubly stochastic case is essentially trivial and sidesteps the central relaxation of the paper; (ii) the scenario where the number of neighbors remains unchanged over time (is this out-neighbors or in-neighbors?) feels contrived. A concrete, nontrivial construction demonstrating when the assumption is satisfied would be necessary.

- Limited clarity on the benefits of the absolute probability sequence approach: It is unclear how this technique improves over classical analyses (e.g., [52]) for push-SA. Regarding convergence to a weighted average with row-stochastic mixing, related insights already exist for static graphs, such as:
  – J. Chen, A. Sayed, “Distributed Pareto Optimization via Diffusion Strategies,” IEEE Trans. Signal Processing, 2013.

- Presentation gaps in the finite-time results: The theorems do not provide the detail typically expected in finite-time analyses. For example, stating that “C2 depends on L, γ_min, γ_max, A_max, b_max …” does not indicate the scale of the bound; without explicit dependence, the bound in (7) may be practically uninformative if C2 is large. The push-SA result (Theorem 5) is only presented for time-varying stepsizes, and the statement that “our current analysis approach cannot be directly applied for this case” is not further elaborated. Additionally, in Theorem 3, it is unclear how the convergence rate of η_t influences the finite-time MSE under constant stepsizes.

Post Rebuttal:
I appreciate the authors’ detailed response, but my original concerns remain. A simulation-based demonstration is unlikely to be persuasive because many relevant scenarios may violate Assumption 6. An analytical example clarifying the necessity of Assumption 6 would be more compelling. In essence, Assumption 6 and the analysis based on the absolute probability sequence are central to the claimed novelty, yet the demonstrated applicability seems limited to well-known cases.

Furthermore, while C2 being large may be acceptable, it is important to specify exactly how C2 scales with L, γ_min, γ_max, A_max, and b_max. The current exposition avoids detailing this dependence.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
No obvious negative societal impacts. Limitations and suggested improvements are covered in the main review.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the convergence distributed linear SA under Markovian sampling. Both asymptotic and non-asymptotic convergence rate results are provided. This paper consider the setting in which the agents interact over a time-varying directed graph, which extend previous studies. Overall the results in this paper is solid and has some technical contributions.
reviews.main_review:
Overall I think the results in this paper is solid and should be some interesting to the community. I have the following comments about the paper.

(1) Only convergence rate is characterized in the main theorems. It would be better to see the sample complexity result (total number of samples that required to achieve $\epsilon$-accuracy level) so that the readers can compare the contributions in this paper with the state-of-art.

(2) Although it is a theoretical paper, empirical verification is also suggested even with toy example.

(3) As claimed by the author, the dependence on some conditional numbers is not clear. However those conditional numbers could affects the convergence rate significantly. A detailed discussion about those numbers would makes the paper more convincing. 

-------- after rebuttal ---------
In the discussion, since the area chair and reviewer AANE raised some concerns about assumptions and the author did not address these concerns convincingly, I change my score to weakly reject.

reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper analyzes the convergence of distributed linear stochastic approximation under Markovian sampling, providing both asymptotic and finite-time rates. The setting allows agents to communicate over a time-varying directed network, extending prior work. Overall, the results appear technically sound and constitute a meaningful contribution.

Main Review:
I find the results solid and likely of interest to the community. That said, I have several comments:

1) The main theorems focus on convergence rates. It would be valuable to also present sample complexity bounds (i.e., the total number of samples needed to reach an epsilon-accurate solution) to facilitate comparison with the current state of the art.

2) Even though the paper is primarily theoretical, some empirical validation—perhaps on a simple toy problem—would strengthen the work.

3) As the authors themselves note, the dependence on certain condition numbers is not clearly specified. Since these quantities can substantially influence the rates, a more thorough discussion of their roles would make the claims more convincing.

After Rebuttal:
Following the discussion, the area chair and reviewer AANE expressed concerns about the assumptions, and the authors did not address these issues convincingly. Accordingly, I am revising my recommendation to a weak reject.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
I do not see potential negative societal impacts arising from this work.

title: Local $K$-means: An Efficient Optimization Algorithm And Its Generalization

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a local efficient version of k-means which leverages the KNN graph of the data to compute neighborhoods of data. Extensive empirical results both on synthetic and real data are reported.
reviews.main_review:
Running times are not reported for the results on real data in Table 3. One of the main advantages of the proposed method is gained efficiency with respect to the clustering competitors, therefore running times should be reported and discussed. 

The proposed method LKM also depends on the initialization of the clustering y, and this is not discussed. It's unclear whether the results on simulated data in Table 1 are averages of different runs or not. This is not discussed and it should. Results should be averages and standard deviations should also be reported.

It is well-known that k-means is sensitive to outliers, but it's unclear in Figure 3(b) why the performance of k-means is poor in this case. It seems it was still able to detect the four clusters in the data?

In Section 3, the definition of neighborhood of a point is confusing and not necessarily correct. The notion of neighbor is not symmetric: x_j may be a neighbor of x_i, but not vice versa. 

I don't find the analogy of KNN graph with social networks particularly useful or insightful. They are not quite the same concept, really. The paper can instead be improved by discussing related work on exemplar-based approaches aimed at making clustering more robust. See for example recent work on regularized NMF methods which leverage the KNN graphs of the data, e.g. Mani et al, Unsupervised Selective Manifold Regularized Matrix Factorization, SDM 2021, and related work.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitations related to the construction of the KNN graph are discussed. No potential negative societal impact is expected.

The proposed method LKM also depends on the initialization of the clustering y, and this is not discussed. It's unclear whether the results on simulated data in Table 1 are averages of different runs or not. This is not discussed and it should. Results should be averages and standard deviations should also be reported.

It is well-known that k-means is sensitive to outliers, but it's unclear in Figure 3(b) why the performance of k-means is poor in this case. It seems it was still able to detect the four clusters in the data?

In Section 3, the definition of neighborhood of a point is confusing and not necessarily correct. The notion of neighbor is not symmetric: x_j may be a neighbor of x_i, but not vice versa. 

I don't find the analogy of KNN graph with social networks particularly useful or insightful. They are not quite the same concept, really. The paper can instead be improved by discussing related work on exemplar-based approaches aimed at making clustering more robust. See for example recent work on regularized NMF methods which leverage the KNN graphs of the data, e.g. Mani et al, Unsupervised Selective Manifold Regularized Matrix Factorization, SDM 2021, and related work.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitations related to the construction of the KNN graph are discussed. No potential negative societal impact is expected.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper proposes a locally efficient variant of k-means that exploits the data’s KNN graph to define neighborhoods, and reports extensive experiments on both synthetic and real datasets.

Main Review:
- Table 3 omits runtime measurements for the real-data experiments. Since computational efficiency is a central claimed benefit over competing clustering methods, the paper should include and analyze wall-clock times for these results.
- The performance of LKM appears dependent on the initialization of the cluster labels y, but this aspect is not addressed. It is also unclear whether the simulated results in Table 1 are averaged over multiple runs. The paper should clarify this, report averages, and include standard deviations.
- While k-means is known to be sensitive to outliers, the rationale for its weak performance in Figure 3(b) is not evident. Visually, it seems to still identify four clusters, so the degradation needs explanation.
- In Section 3, the neighborhood definition is confusing and may be problematic. The neighbor relation is not symmetric: x_j can be a neighbor of x_i without the reverse being true.
- The comparison between KNN graphs and social networks is not particularly illuminating; the concepts differ in important ways. A more useful improvement would be to expand the discussion of related work on exemplar-based and robustness-focused approaches. For instance, see Mani et al., “Unsupervised Selective Manifold Regularized Matrix Factorization,” SDM 2021, and related literature leveraging KNN graphs.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The paper appropriately notes limitations tied to KNN graph construction. No adverse societal impacts are anticipated.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Decision:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This submission introduces a computationally efficient clustering algorithm called LKM, which focuses on the scenarios where data is presented in the form of graph. In brief, only the k-nearest neighbour distances of each point are considered in the objective and a simple truncation of large distances is introduced to mitigate the effect of outliers.  The method is shown to produce promising practical performance in comparison with existing techniques both computationally and in terms of clustering performance.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

LKM is developed based on the k-means problem which is a fundamental problem in machine learning and the authors might bring some new insights and develop a new algorithm. The main strength of LKM lies in the practical aspects of the proposed algorithm, in that it is efficient and the performance is compelling. 
Specifically, the computational overhead is O(nk), where n and k denote the number of samples and nearest neighbors, respectively.

In addition, the unified framework of KSUMSC, and LCM also seems interesting. It revolves that SC, a popular clustering algorithm derived from graph cuts, can be optimized with the same time complexity as that of LKM.

Weaknesses:
1. Line 9: "Specifically....are denote the...". The sentence is grammatically incorrect, "are" should be removed.

2. Some works relating to the connection between k-means and clustering with graph cuts are not discussed in Section 2 (Related work).

3. The performance of SC optimized by the proposed algorithm is not shown.

4. What does \bar{y} stand for in Eq. (15) and (16)?

5. e_i should be a column vector.

6. Some steps in the derivations are not explained. What is the connection between (11) and (17)? 

7. The evaluation needs to be improved. The proposed clustering method uses ball-tree to construct a k-nearest neighbors graph as an intermediate step. Correspondingly, it is suggested to include the clustering performance of ball-tree as a baseline to compare.

8. Have the paper proof-read to remove grammatical errors

9. In page 7, line 178, "#Iter" should be "#Iter.".

10. In page 7, table 2, "FLK" should be "LKM".

11. References should be shown in the same format.

**comment after rebuttal**
I acknowledge that I read and other reviewers' comments and authors' responses. I would like to keep my score as most concerns of mine have been well addressed.

reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper presents LKM, a fast clustering algorithm tailored to graph-based data. The key idea is to restrict the objective to each point’s k-nearest neighbor distances and to truncate large distances to reduce the influence of outliers. Empirically, the method shows strong practical results relative to existing approaches, both in speed and clustering quality.

Main Review:
Strengths:
- The method builds on the classic k-means problem, a foundational ML task, and has the potential to provide fresh insights and a new algorithmic direction. Its main merit is practical: it is computationally efficient and delivers compelling empirical performance.
- The computational cost is O(nk), where n is the number of data points and k is the number of nearest neighbors.
- The unified perspective linking KSUMSC and LCM is also noteworthy. It indicates that spectral clustering (SC), derived from graph cuts, can be optimized with the same time complexity as LKM.

Weaknesses:
1. Line 9 contains a grammatical error: “Specifically... are denote the...” — remove “are.”
2. Section 2 (Related Work) omits discussion of prior work on connections between k-means and graph-cut-based clustering.
3. The paper does not report the performance of SC when optimized using the proposed approach.
4. Clarify the meaning of \bar{y} in Eqs. (15) and (16).
5. e_i should be a column vector.
6. Some derivation steps are insufficiently explained. Please clarify the link between equations (11) and (17).
7. The evaluation could be strengthened. Since the method uses a ball-tree to build the k-NN graph as an intermediate step, please include the ball-tree’s clustering performance as a baseline for comparison.
8. The manuscript should be proofread to correct grammatical issues.
9. Page 7, line 178: change “#Iter” to “#Iter.”
10. Page 7, Table 2: “FLK” should read “LKM.”
11. Ensure the references follow a consistent format.

Comment after rebuttal:
I have read the authors’ response and the other reviews. I am maintaining my score, as most of my key concerns have been satisfactorily addressed.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
Yes.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

Original Decision (Rating)
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: The authors use a reformulation of the k-means objective to propose an algorithm that minimizes that objective (or some relaxation thereof), and then show some experiments that indicate promising clustering performance.  Currently I believe the paper needs revision before acceptance: the empirical results are not particularly compelling as they omit runtimes on real-world datasets, and the theoretical results do not factor in or bound the number of iterations of the algorithm.
reviews.main_review:
Hello authors,

Thank you for your submission.  I enjoyed reading it and I think there are some nice ideas contained in the paper.  However, I have a number of concerns that keep me from recommending acceptance for this paper.  Primarily, my biggest concern is the given runtime bound (which I find inaccurate as it does not count the number of iterations), and the experimental evaluation: runtime analysis is a very important measure of the utility of an algorithm, and this is missing for the real-world datasets.

More details below (in no particular order):

1. The computational complexity does not consider the number of iterations before convergence.  Therefore I find the claim that the algorithm takes $O(nc)$ time (or rather $O(n(k + c))$ where $c$ is the number of clusters and $k$ is the number of distances known per point) misleading or incorrect.  In fact, Lloyd's algorithm also takes $O(nc)$ per iteration, but the number of iterations is not easily bounded.  Unless the authors can conclusively show that the number of iterations should not be considered in the runtime, this claim must be modified or retracted.

2. As pointed out in the introduction, the initialization of k-means can make a huge difference for performance.  However, on line 204 the authors simply write "K-means is initialized in a random way"---but what is this random way?  If I were to, e.g., randomly assign points to clusters to initialize, this can lead to very poor results and slow convergence, at least for Lloyd's algorithm.  Even
running many trials of this strategy will produce poor performance for every strategy.  Thus proper initialization is very important.  It would be very helpful if the authors could provide more information on this point.

3. The authors have not provided any information on runtime on real-world datasets.  Thus it is difficult to evaluate the utility of the proposed algorithm, LKM.  Clustering algorithms often behave very differently on diverse datasets, and thus I think that runtimes only on the toy dataset---and only comparing with Lloyd's algorithm---does not give a full picture.  In fact, the number of iterations on the toy datasets is very small when compared to what Lloyd's algorithm will do on higher-dimensional real datasets (where cluster boundaries are not so clear-cut).  For the sake of clarity, the authors should address the issue of runtime comprehensively on real-world datasets.

4. It would also be nice to include some information on the dimensionality and number of points in each dataset listed in 4.2.1 in the main paper, if possible.  I appreciate that 16 datasets are used---this seems like a fairly comprehensive set of experiments---but I suspect most readers will not have an idea of what the characteristics of these datasets are, and many will not look at the
supplemental material.  When I consult the supplementary material, I see that many of the datasets used are relatively high-dimensional; the minimum dimension appears to be 256.  I would suggest that the authors also try on lower-dimensional data (e.g. below 50 dimensions), as convergence characteristics of different algorithms tend to be very different in lower dimensions.

5. A note about the limitations: there are many techniques to quickly construct k-NN graphs.  In lower dimensions, this can be done via trees (see, e.g., [1-3]); in higher dimensions, this can be done approximately via hashing (e.g., [4-5]).  In fact even if only a similarity function is used, so long as that function is a kernel, additional branch-and-bound techniques could be used ([6-8]).  Anyway, perhaps you will find these interesting, if you were not already aware of those lines of work.

6. When comparing Lloyd's algorithm to LKM, note also that there are several variants of Lloyd's algorithm that produce exactly the same result in accelerated time.  The authors even cite some (reference [11]).  There are more however, especially Elkan's [9] and Hamerly's [10] that perform very well in practice that should probably be considered in an empirical comparison.  Personally, if I am doing k-means in practice, I often don't bother with the standard Lloyd algorithm because Elkan's or Hamerly's variants (or the
tree-based ones) are so much faster---it can often be an orders-of-magnitude speedup.

7. Can more detail be provided in Algorithm 1 on how y is initialized randomly?  Is the algorithm uniformly randomly drawing a cluster assignment for each point?

8. I think the paper could benefit from some careful proofreading.  There are numerous grammatical issues, especially having to do with tenses (present/past/etc.).

9. The suggestion in Eq. (10) to convert a (non-metric) similarity function to a dissimilarity function still produces a non-metric: it is not guaranteed to satisfy the triangle inequality.  This makes the change from Eq. (8) (which is built on distances using D) to Eq. (11) (which is built on dissimilarities using G) significant: particularly, I am no longer certain that we are solving the same problem as (3).  I am okay with the fact that LKM solves a relaxation of (3) but I think this should be explicitly pointed out.  On the other hand, note that if the similarity function is a kernel, then it *does* induce a distance metric in kernel space, and that could be used instead.  Specifically, Eq. (2.2) in [8] uses this induced distance metric.

[1] "N-Body'problems in statistical learning", NIPS 2001
[2] "Tree-independent dual-tree algorithms", ICML 2013
[3] "Random projection trees and low dimensional manifolds", STOC 2008
[4] "Near-optimal hashing algorithms for approximate nearest neighbor in high
dimensions", FOCS '06
[5] "LSH Forest: Practical Algorithms Made Theoretical", SODA 2017
[6] "Asymmetric LSH (ALSH) for sublinear time maximum inner product search (MIPS)", NIPS 2014
[7] "Maximum inner-product search using cone trees", KDD 2012
[8] "Fast Exact Max-Kernel Search", SDM 2013
[9] "Using the triangle inequality to accelerate k-means", ICML 2003
[10] "Making k-means even faster", SDM 2010

I hope that these comments are helpful!
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I don't think that a new clustering algorithm has either positive or negative societal impact.  So everything seems fine here.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

[1] "N-Body'problems in statistical learning", NIPS 2001
[2] "Tree-independent dual-tree algorithms", ICML 2013
[3] "Random projection trees and low dimensional manifolds", STOC 2008
[4] "Near-optimal hashing algorithms for approximate nearest neighbor in high
dimensions", FOCS '06
[5] "LSH Forest: Practical Algorithms Made Theoretical", SODA 2017
[6] "Asymmetric LSH (ALSH) for sublinear time maximum inner product search (MIPS)", NIPS 2014

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper reformulates the k-means objective and proposes an algorithm to minimize that objective (or a relaxation of it), followed by experiments suggesting encouraging clustering performance. In its current form, I believe the paper requires revision before it can be accepted: the empirical evidence is not persuasive without runtime measurements on real-world datasets, and the theoretical analysis omits any accounting or bound on the number of iterations.

Main Review:
Dear authors,

Thank you for the submission. I found the paper engaging and containing several appealing ideas. That said, I have significant reservations that prevent me from recommending acceptance. The primary issues are the stated runtime bound—which I view as inaccurate because it ignores the iteration count—and the experimental evaluation, which lacks runtime analysis on real datasets. Since runtime is crucial for assessing an algorithm’s practical utility, this omission is serious.

Detailed comments (unordered):

1. The complexity analysis does not include the number of iterations required for convergence. Consequently, asserting that the algorithm runs in O(nc) time (or O(n(k + c)), where c is the number of clusters and k is the number of known distances per point) is, in my view, misleading or incorrect. Lloyd’s algorithm also achieves O(nc) per iteration, but its iteration count is not straightforward to bound. Unless you can convincingly argue that iteration counts can be excluded from the runtime, this claim should be revised or withdrawn.

2. The introduction emphasizes that k-means initialization substantially affects performance, yet at line 204 you state only that “K-means is initialized in a random way.” What specific randomization procedure is used? For instance, randomly assigning points to clusters can lead to poor solutions and slow convergence for Lloyd’s method; multiple restarts of such a naive strategy typically still underperform. Proper initialization is critical—please elaborate on your approach.

3. There are no runtime results reported for real-world datasets, making it difficult to judge the practicality of LKM. Clustering methods can behave quite differently across datasets; therefore, runtimes limited to a toy dataset—and only against standard Lloyd’s—do not provide a complete picture. Moreover, iteration counts on the toy problems are small relative to what Lloyd’s typically requires on high-dimensional, less well-separated data. Please provide a thorough runtime study on real-world datasets.

4. For the datasets in Section 4.2.1, it would be helpful to include the dimensionality and sample counts in the main paper. While I appreciate the breadth of the 16 datasets, many readers will not know their characteristics and may not consult the supplement. From the supplement, many datasets appear relatively high-dimensional (with a minimum around 256). I recommend also evaluating lower-dimensional datasets (e.g., under 50 dimensions), as convergence behavior can differ substantially in lower dimensions.

5. Regarding limitations: there exist numerous methods to build k-NN graphs efficiently. In low dimensions, tree-based approaches are effective [1–3]; in high dimensions, approximate methods based on hashing are available [4–5]. If the similarity function is a kernel, additional branch-and-bound techniques can be applied [6–8]. You may find these references useful if not already familiar.

6. In comparing Lloyd’s algorithm with LKM, note that several accelerated variants produce identical solutions much faster. You cite some (e.g., your ref. [11]), but there are notable practical performers such as Elkan’s method [9] and Hamerly’s method [10], which should be included in empirical comparisons. In practice, I rarely use vanilla Lloyd’s because Elkan/Hamerly (and tree-based methods) often yield orders-of-magnitude speedups.

7. Algorithm 1 lacks specifics on how y is randomly initialized. Does the procedure assign each point to a cluster uniformly at random?

8. The manuscript would benefit from careful proofreading. There are multiple grammatical issues, particularly with verb tense usage.

9. The transformation in Eq. (10) from similarity to dissimilarity does not ensure a metric; the triangle inequality may fail. This makes the shift from Eq. (8) (distance-based with D) to Eq. (11) (dissimilarity-based with G) nontrivial, and it is unclear that the same problem as (3) is being solved. I am comfortable with LKM targeting a relaxation of (3), but this should be stated explicitly. Alternatively, if the similarity is a kernel, it induces a valid metric in feature space that could be used instead; see Eq. (2.2) in [8].

[1] “N-Body problems in statistical learning,” NIPS 2001
[2] “Tree-independent dual-tree algorithms,” ICML 2013
[3] “Random projection trees and low dimensional manifolds,” STOC 2008
[4] “Near-optimal hashing algorithms for approximate nearest neighbor in high dimensions,” FOCS 2006
[5] “LSH Forest: Practical Algorithms Made Theoretical,” SODA 2017
[6] “Asymmetric LSH (ALSH) for sublinear time maximum inner product search (MIPS),” NIPS 2014
[7] “Maximum inner-product search using cone trees,” KDD 2012
[8] “Fast Exact Max-Kernel Search,” SDM 2013
[9] “Using the triangle inequality to accelerate k-means,” ICML 2003
[10] “Making k-means even faster,” SDM 2010

I hope these comments are useful.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see notable positive or negative societal impacts from proposing a new clustering method; no issues here.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

Original Decision (Rating)
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

Summary:
The authors use a reformulation of the k-means objective to propose an algorithm that minimizes that objective (or some relaxation thereof), and then show some experiments that indicate promising clustering performance.  Currently I believe the paper needs revision before acceptance: the empirical results are not particularly compelling as they omit runtimes on real-world datasets, and the theoretical results do not factor in or bound the number of iterations of the algorithm.

Main Review:
Hello authors,

Thank you for your submission.  I enjoyed reading it and I think there are some nice ideas contained in the paper.  However, I have a number of concerns that keep me from recommending acceptance for this paper.  Primarily, my biggest concern is the given runtime bound (which I find inaccurate as it does not count the number of iterations), and the experimental evaluation: runtime analysis is a very important measure of the utility of an algorithm, and this is missing for the real-world datasets.

More details below (in no particular order):

1. The computational complexity does not consider the number of iterations before convergence.  Therefore I find the claim that the algorithm takes $O(nc)$ time (or rather $O(n(k + c))$ where $c$ is the number of clusters and $k$ is the number of distances known per point) misleading or incorrect.  In fact, Lloyd's algorithm also takes $O(nc)$ per iteration, but the number of iterations is not easily bounded.  Unless the authors can conclusively show that the number of iterations should not be considered in the runtime, this claim must be modified or retracted.

2. As pointed out in the introduction, the initialization of k-means can make a huge difference for performance.  However, on line 204 the authors simply write "K-means is initialized in a random way"---but what is this random way?  If I were to, e.g., randomly assign points to clusters to initialize, this can lead to very poor results and slow convergence, at least for Lloyd's algorithm.  Even
running many trials of this strategy will produce poor performance for every strategy.  Thus proper initialization is very important.  It would be very helpful if the authors could provide more information on this point.

3. The authors have not provided any information on runtime on real-world datasets.  Thus it is difficult to evaluate the utility of the proposed algorithm, LKM.  Clustering algorithms often behave very differently on diverse datasets, and thus I think that runtimes only on the toy dataset---and only comparing with Lloyd's algorithm---does not give a full picture.  In fact, the number of iterations on the toy datasets is very small when compared to what Lloyd's algorithm will do on higher-dimensional real datasets (where cluster boundaries are not so clear-cut).  For the sake of clarity, the authors should address the issue of runtime comprehensively on real-world datasets.

4. It would also be nice to include some information on the dimensionality and number of points in each dataset listed in 4.2.1 in the main paper, if possible.  I appreciate that 16 datasets are used---this seems like a fairly comprehensive set of experiments---but I suspect most readers will not have an idea of what the characteristics of these datasets are, and many will not look at the
supplemental material.  When I consult the supplementary material, I see that many of the datasets used are relatively high-dimensional; the minimum dimension appears to be 256.  I would suggest that the authors also try on lower-dimensional data (e.g. below 50 dimensions), as convergence characteristics of different algorithms tend to be very different in lower dimensions.

5. A note about the limitations: there are many techniques to quickly construct k-NN graphs.  In lower dimensions, this can be done via trees (see, e.g., [1-3]); in higher dimensions, this can be done approximately via hashing (e.g., [4-5]).  In fact even if only a similarity function is used, so long as that function is a kernel, additional branch-and-bound techniques could be used ([6-8]).  Anyway, perhaps you will find these interesting, if you were not already aware of those lines of work.

6. When comparing Lloyd's algorithm to LKM, note also that there are several variants of Lloyd's algorithm that produce exactly the same result in accelerated time.  The authors even cite some (reference [11]).  There are more however, especially Elkan's [9] and Hamerly's [10] that perform very well in practice that should probably be considered in an empirical comparison.  Personally, if I am doing k-means in practice, I often don't bother with the standard Lloyd algorithm because Elkan's or Hamerly's variants (or the
tree-based ones) are so much faster---it can often be an orders-of-magnitude speedup.

7. Can more detail be provided in Algorithm 1 on how y is initialized randomly?  Is the algorithm uniformly randomly drawing a cluster assignment for each point?

8. I think the paper could benefit from some careful proofreading.  There are numerous grammatical issues, especially having to do with tenses (present/past/etc.).

9. The suggestion in Eq. (10) to convert a (non-metric) similarity function to a dissimilarity function still produces a non-metric: it is not guaranteed to satisfy the triangle inequality.  This makes the change from Eq. (8) (which is built on distances using D) to Eq. (11) (which is built on dissimilarities using G) significant: particularly, I am no longer certain that we are solving the same problem as (3).  I am okay with the fact that LKM solves a relaxation of (3) but I think this should be explicitly pointed out.  On the other hand, note that if the similarity function is a kernel, then it *does* induce a distance metric in kernel space, and that could be used instead.  Specifically, Eq. (2.2) in [8] uses this induced distance metric.

[1] "N-Body'problems in statistical learning", NIPS 2001
[2] "Tree-independent dual-tree algorithms", ICML 2013
[3] "Random projection trees and low dimensional manifolds", STOC 2008
[4] "Near-optimal hashing algorithms for approximate nearest neighbor in high
dimensions", FOCS '06
[5] "LSH Forest: Practical Algorithms Made Theoretical", SODA 2017
[6] "Asymmetric LSH (ALSH) for sublinear time maximum inner product search (MIPS)", NIPS 2014
[7] "Maximum inner-product search using cone trees", KDD 2012
[8] "Fast Exact Max-Kernel Search", SDM 2013
[9] "Using the triangle inequality to accelerate k-means", ICML 2003
[10] "Making k-means even faster", SDM 2010

I hope that these comments are helpful!

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I don't think that a new clustering algorithm has either positive or negative societal impact.  So everything seems fine here.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.original_rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

reviews.summary: This paper considers a variant of k-means which takes the k-nearest neighbor graph as input. The proposed algorithm, called local k-means (LKM in short), then works by locally optimizing the assignment vector of a point using an objective function that is roughly the incremental cost for the k-means objective. The paper provided a lot of empirical data to show the advantages of LKM.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, this paper proposes a novel k-means algorithm, but I think there has not been enough discussion on its guarantees (from both a theoretical and empircal point of view) to make it convincing. The paper writing can also be greatly improved.

Originality: The mthods used in this paper are new. The paper converts the k-means objective into a different format which is compatible with nearest-neighbor graph inputs, where the main variable is the indicator matrix, and optimizes the columns in this matrix one by one (each column corresponding to the assignment vector of one point). The idea of optimizing the assignment of each point to clusters might not be new to the community, but the proposed local optization function seems to be novel.

Quality: The paper does not provide any theoretical guarantees for the proposed algorithm and mainly used empirical evaluation approaches to show its superiority. The experiments are carefully designed, but I think the authors are trying to make some claims that are too broad and thus the provided empirical results do not seem to be convincing enough to show that this approach really beats state-of-the-art. LKM is compared with a lot of baselines and that may still not cover the entire state-of-the-art clustering approaches. Some comments:
- The self-synthesized data are well designed, and serves to show that LKM performs well on some datasets whose natural clustering cannot be captured by the optimal k-means objective function. However, it seems to me that they might be suitable for other types of clustering such as density-based clustering (DBSCAN might be good for "Mickey"), or common k-means implementation packages might also be good for them (k-means++ might be good for "Outlier" depending on the sampled seeds?).  I wonder if the authors have considered these?
- After reading the experiment section I get a bit more confused about the claims that this paper is trying to make: LKM seems to be beating most baselines on the datasets in clustering quality and be significantly faster than k-means? The improvement of LKM , especially when compared with KSUMS on most of the datasets, do not seem to be very significant. Actually, the results gave me the impression that there are many datasets and each of them might be suitable for different types of clustering methods, with LKM performing relative well for all datasets.

Clarity: The writing clarity is fine, although occasionally there are some flaws such as definition/notation that has not been used again later, typos and so on, but it does not affect reading too much. It has a clear problem definition and algorithm description. That being said, I'm not very impressed with the organization of the paper. 
- The paper spent enough space on problem definition but the paragraphs did not serve to fully motivate this design. For example, previous methods like KSUMS and LKM, whose math formulation, as the authors pointed out, have very similar structure with the one used in this paper, but the authors did not provide intuition about why the differences lead to better performance.
- The description of the algorithm design is only about one page and I think there should be more intuitive explanation of why this particular design of local objective function (18) might lead to a better solution and make it robust against outliers. 

Significance: This paper could potentially propose a solution that advances state-of-the-art, but the comparsion between the new algorithm and previous approaches does not seem to convince people of its superiority. Also, I'm not sure if the improvement is significant enough for this venue.

Minor comments:
- In abstract, line 5, the authors mentioned "KSUMS and SC" without citation and before defining which algorithms these are referring to.
- line 26, "For matrix A, we call it indicator matrix", I don't really see the point of introducing the indicator matrix here, and the notation is not really used again in this paper.
- line 86, "where diag(A) = [a11,..., ann]^T",  A is not used in (7), and I'm not sure about what a11, ..., ann are referring to.
- In (9), I'm not sure about why is d_ij^{(k)} set as \gamma otherwise? Can the authors comment on this?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors have adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

The authors have adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

title: Proximal and Federated Random Reshuffling

reviews.summary: This paper studies a proximal extension of random reshuffling algorithm, namely ProxRR for finite-sum composite optimization. ProxRR requires only one proximal steps per round, which reduces the complexities incurred by costly proximal operators. This paper also studies a federated version of ProxRR, namely FedRR, and proved theoretical advantages over Local SGD.
reviews.main_review:
I would like to express my appreciation for your submission to NeurIPS 2021. The paper is overall well-written and I enjoyed reading it. My concerns are as follows.

1. The ProxRR presented in this paper applies only one proximal step per epoch. This reduces the number of calls to proximal operators, but at the same time incurs additional complexities (say, in eq (6)). It is hard to calibrate the significance of ProxRR bound (6) with [1] or Proximal SGD (7) since there are two factors mixed in --- reshuffling and reduced proximal calls. Have you considered doing ablation study to separate these two factors? For example, how does “ProxRR with proximal at every step” work? Will it eliminate the additional term in (6)?

2. This work compares  FedRR with other smooth federated optimization algorithm such as FedAvg and SCAFFOLD. How does FedRR compare with other federated composite algorithms such as [2] (theoretically or empirically)? For example, for experiments presented in Fig. 2, how does FedRR compare with FedDualAvg [2] if $\lambda_1 \neq 0$?

[1] Random Reshufﬂing: Simple Analysis with Vast Improvements, NeurIPS 2020.
[2] Federated Composite Optimization, ICML 2021.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: See main review for limitations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

Summary:
This paper studies a proximal extension of random reshuffling algorithm, namely ProxRR for finite-sum composite optimization. ProxRR requires only one proximal steps per round, which reduces the complexities incurred by costly proximal operators. This paper also studies a federated version of ProxRR, namely FedRR, and proved theoretical advantages over Local SGD.

Main Review:
